questions_needing_review.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions_needing_review.stem:
A hospital transfusion service medical director reviews the semiannual competency-assessment file of a technologist newly approved to perform antibody identification independently. The file documents: (1) direct observation of the technologist performing routine antibody panel testing, including specimen handling; (2) verification that her recorded and reported results matched the source worksheet; and (3) correct resolution of a blinded internal proficiency sample. During a subsequent inspection, an assessor notes the file contains no documentation of how the technologist would respond when a patient's forward and reverse ABO grouping results fail to match.

Which of the six minimal CLIA competency-assessment elements is missing from this technologist's file?

options.text: Review of intermediate test results, QC, PT, and maintenance records
Monitoring of the recording and reporting of test results
Assessment of performance using previously analyzed or proficiency testing samples
Direct observation of routine test performance
Evaluation of problem-solving skills

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The six minimal CLIA competency-assessment elements include evaluation of the individual's ability to handle discrepancies and technical issues (problem-solving skills). The scenario documents direct observation, accurate recording/reporting, and successful resolution of a blinded PT/previously-analyzed sample, but there is no documentation of the technologist's ability to work through an unexpected discrepancy such as an ABO forward/reverse mismatch — this is specifically what the problem-solving element is designed to capture.

option_explanations.E: Correct: the sources define this CLIA element as evaluating an individual's ability to handle discrepancies and technical issues, which is exactly the gap described (no documentation of ABO discrepancy troubleshooting).
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: already satisfied — the stem states the technologist was directly observed performing routine testing including specimen handling.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: already satisfied — the stem states her recorded/reported results were verified against the source worksheet.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: already satisfied — the stem states she correctly resolved a blinded internal proficiency sample.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this element concerns review of worksheets, QC, PT, and maintenance records generally, not the specific gap described, which is about handling an unexpected discrepancy.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: The six CLIA competency-assessment elements are distinct and non-overlapping; a file with direct observation, record review, and PT performance can still be deficient if it lacks documented evaluation of problem-solving/discrepancy-handling skills.

source_references.supports: Lists the six minimal CLIA competency assessment requirements, including 'Evaluation of problem-solving skills' as a distinct element from direct observation, record monitoring, record review, maintenance observation, and PT/previously-analyzed sample assessment.

source_references.locator: Section 1, 'CLIA Minimal Regulatory Requirements for Competency Assessment' table
source_references.supports: Table of CLIA competency assessment requirements defines 'Problem-Solving Skills' as evaluating the individual's ability to handle discrepancies and technical issues, distinct from the other five elements.

review.notes: The six CLIA elements and their definitions are accurately sourced (SRC-053 Sec.3 list; SRC-027 'CLIA Minimal Regulatory Requirements for Competency Assessment' table, which explicitly defines 'Problem-Solving Skills' as evaluating ability to handle discrepancies and technical issues - matching the ABO forward/reverse mismatch scenario). Items (1)-(3) in the stem cleanly map to three distinct elements: Direct Observation (item 1), Record/Recording Monitoring (item 2), and Test Performance Assessment via PT/previously-analyzed samples (item 3) - correctly eliminating options B, C, D. The intend

_audit.verdict_notes: The six CLIA elements and their definitions are accurately sourced (SRC-053 Sec.3 list; SRC-027 'CLIA Minimal Regulatory Requirements for Competency Assessment' table, which explicitly defines 'Problem-Solving Skills' as evaluating ability to handle discrepancies and technical issues - matching the ABO forward/reverse mismatch scenario). Items (1)-(3) in the stem cleanly map to three distinct elements: Direct Observation (item 1), Record/Recording Monitoring (item 2), and Test Performance Assessment via PT/previously-analyzed samples (item 3) - correctly eliminating options B, C, D. The intended answer (A, problem-solving) is clearly signaled by the assessor's explicit note about undocumented discrepancy-handling. However, strictly by elimination, a fourth element - 'Review of intermediate test results, QC, PT, and maintenance records' (option E) - is also never confirmed as documented in the file (item 2 maps to 'Record Monitoring,' a distinct element from 'Review of Records' per the SRC-027 table), and 'Maintenance Observation' (not offered as an option) is likewise unconfirmed. Because the stem's three documented items do not affirmatively rule out E, a rigorous test-taker doing strict elimination could argue E is also 'missing' from the file, creating a secondary defensible reading even though A is the clearly intended and best-supported answer given the assessor's specific flagged gap.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 58-year-old man with multiple myeloma in partial remission after induction chemotherapy is scheduled for autologous hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation. He receives filgrastim (G-CSF) for progenitor cell mobilization, but his peripheral blood CD34+ cell count remains inadequate on day 4 of mobilization. Plerixafor is added to the regimen, after which the circulating CD34+ cell count rises enough to permit a successful apheresis collection. What is the mechanism by which plerixafor increases the number of circulating hematopoietic progenitor cells in this setting?

options.text: It binds erythropoietin receptors on erythroid progenitors to directly stimulate red cell production
It antagonizes the CXCR4 receptor, disrupting the interaction that retains progenitor cells within the bone marrow niche
It irradiates contaminating lymphocytes within the collected product to reduce graft-versus-host disease risk
It increases red cell 2,3-DPG levels, shifting the oxygen dissociation curve rightward to enhance tissue oxygen delivery
It cross-links nucleic acids within residual pathogens to prevent their replication, a pathogen-reduction mechanism

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The approved sources describe plerixafor as a CXCR4 antagonist used together with G-CSF/filgrastim to mobilize hematopoietic progenitor cells out of the marrow and into the peripheral blood for apheresis collection. By blocking CXCR4, plerixafor disrupts the retention signal that anchors progenitors in the marrow niche, increasing the circulating CD34+ yield in a poor mobilizer such as this myeloma patient.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. This conflates plerixafor/G-CSF (hematopoietic growth factors used for stem cell mobilization) with erythropoiesis-stimulating mechanisms; the sources describe plerixafor only as a CXCR4 antagonist that mobilizes progenitors, with no erythropoietin-receptor activity described.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Increased 2,3-DPG is a physiologic compensatory adaptation to anemia that shifts the oxygen dissociation curve to the right to facilitate oxygen release to tissues; it is not a mechanism of plerixafor or any HPC mobilization agent.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Plerixafor is explicitly identified as a CXCR4 antagonist used with G-CSF/filgrastim to move HPCs from the marrow into the peripheral blood for collection.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Nucleic acid cross-linking upon UV exposure is the pathogen-inactivation mechanism of amotosalen+UVA (INTERCEPT) technology used for platelets and plasma, not a mechanism of plerixafor or progenitor mobilization.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. HPC grafts must never be irradiated or leukocyte reduced, as this would destroy the graft's ability to engraft; irradiation is reserved for standard cellular blood components given to the recipient, not the collected progenitor product, and is not a mechanism of plerixafor.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Growth-factor-based HPC mobilization (G-CSF plus the CXCR4 antagonist plerixafor) works by disrupting marrow retention signals to release progenitors into peripheral blood; this mechanism is distinct from erythropoiesis-stimulating action, physiologic oxygen-delivery adaptation to anemia, or pathogen-inactivation chemistry, and the HPC graft itself must never be irradiated.

source_references.locator: Management - Mobilization; Clinical Presentations - Malignant Indications; Common Board Traps - Irradiation Error
source_references.supports: States plerixafor is a CXCR4 antagonist used with G-CSF/filgrastim for HPC mobilization to peripheral blood; lists multiple myeloma as a malignant HSCT indication; states the HPC graft must never be irradiated.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 24 - Management: Mobilization; Prevention: Processing Rules
source_references.supports: Corroborates that G-CSF (Filgrastim) or Plerixafor (CXCR4 antagonist) is used to move stem cells into peripheral blood for collection, and reiterates HPC grafts should never be irradiated.

source_references.locator: Node 46 - Mechanics of the Shift; Node 48 - Amotosalen + UVA (INTERCEPT)
source_references.supports: Provides the 2,3-DPG/oxygen-dissociation-curve compensatory mechanism (used to build the distractor about physiologic adaptation to anemia) and the amotosalen+UVA nucleic-acid cross-linking mechanism (used to build the pathogen-inactivation distractor).

Distractor B (2,3-DPG/oxygen-curve) is directly and accurately drawn from SRC-002 Node 46. Distractor D (amotosalen+UVA nucleic-acid cross-linking) is directly and accurately drawn from SRC-002 Node 48. Distractor E is directly supported by the explicit 'HPC graft must NEVER be irradiated' rule in both SRC-038 a

_audit.verdict_notes: CORE MECHANISM FACTS ARE GROUNDED: SRC-038 and SRC-040 both explicitly identify plerixafor as 'a CXCR4 antagonist' used together with G-CSF/filgrastim to move HPCs from marrow to peripheral blood for collection, and both explicitly state multiple myeloma is a malignant HSCT indication. Distractor B (2,3-DPG/oxygen-curve) is directly and accurately drawn from SRC-002 Node 46. Distractor D (amotosalen+UVA nucleic-acid cross-linking) is directly and accurately drawn from SRC-002 Node 48. Distractor E is directly supported by the explicit 'HPC graft must NEVER be irradiated' rule in both SRC-038 and SRC-040. Distractor A's rationale accurately states sources describe no EPO-receptor activity for plerixafor. However, two concerns: (1) UNSUPPORTED ELABORATION - the credited explanation states plerixafor 'disrupts the retention signal that anchors progenitors in the marrow niche' and option C's text similarly frames the mechanism as 'disrupting the interaction that retains progenitor cells within the bone marrow niche.' None of the four sources describe the CXCR4/SDF-1(CXCL12) retention-signal biology at all - they only label plerixafor 'a CXCR4 antagonist' used for mobilization, without explaining why CXCR4 antagonism causes mobilization. This is standard/true pharmacology but is an inference beyond what these specific sources state, consistent with the question author's own 'moderate' support_confidence rating. (2) BLUEPRINT MISMATCH (the more serious issue) - the targeted node is 'Anemia in Oncology Patients > Management > Growth Factors.' In BB/TM board content, 'growth factors' under an anemia-management heading conventionally means erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (epoetin alfa/darbepoetin) used to manage chemotherapy-induced anemia - a topic with its own well-known safety issues (thrombosis risk, iron repletion before starting, Hgb target ceilings, tumor-progression concerns). This question instead tests G-CSF/plerixafor-based CD34+ stem-cell mobilization for autologous HPC transplant collection - a myeloid/stem-cell-transplant topic entirely unrelated to erythropoiesis or RBC-focused anemia management. None of the four source documents contain any content on erythropoietin/ESA management of oncology-associated anemia; all the HPC/mobilization content sits in the HSCT-specific nodes (SRC-038 Domain VIII, SRC-040 Node 24), not an anemia-management context. The question is well-constructed and internally consistent, but it does not appear to test the specified blueprint node's actual clinical content (ESA management of cancer-associated anemia); it tests a different growth-factor class in a different clinical scenario (transplant mobilization, not anemia treatment). Recommend re-tagging to an HPC-transplantation/mobilization node or replacing with a question on erythropoiesis-stimulating agents to match the stated blueprint node. Answer key (C) is correct and is the single best answer among the options as written.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 22 weeks of gestation is found to have an isolated platelet count of 58,000/uL on routine prenatal screening. She is asymptomatic, blood pressure is normal, and a preconception platelet count one year ago was normal. Peripheral smear shows no schistocytes, coagulation studies are normal, and there is no history of heparin exposure or new medications. A presumptive diagnosis of immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) is made, and HLA and HPA antibody assays are sent as part of the diagnostic workup. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's thrombocytopenia?

options.text: IgG autoantibodies bind platelet surface antigens, resulting in immune-mediated destruction of the antibody-coated platelets
A maternal alloantibody forms against a paternally inherited platelet antigen absent from the mother's own platelets, destroying fetal platelets
Heparin exposure triggers formation of platelet-reactive antibodies that cause pathologic platelet activation and consumption
Deficiency of ADAMTS13 activity leads to accumulation of ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers and microvascular platelet consumption

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The curriculum states that ITP involves platelet destruction, and that HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical to its investigation — reflecting an autoimmune process in which IgG autoantibodies coat the patient's own platelets, targeting them for immune-mediated destruction. This is the mechanism operative in this pregnant patient's own thrombocytopenia, and it is why antibody-directed assays (rather than tests for a consumptive or alloimmune process) are sent to support the diagnosis.

option_explanations.D: Incorrect: ADAMTS13 deficiency/inhibition with resultant microvascular platelet consumption is the mechanism of TTP, which classically presents with microangiopathic hemolysis (schistocytes), neurologic signs, fever, and renal dysfunction — findings absent here and mechanistically distinct from ITP's antibody-mediated platelet destruction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this describes heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), which the curriculum lists as a separate entity from ITP with its own classification and pathogenesis; this patient has no heparin exposure, and HIT's mechanism is not the one being investigated by HLA/HPA antibody testing here.
option_explanations.A: Correct: matches the curriculum's description of ITP as antibody-mediated platelet destruction, with HLA and HPA antibody assays used to investigate the process.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this describes neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT), in which the curriculum identifies HPA-1a as the primary alloantigen target; NAIT is an alloimmune process directed at paternally-derived antigens on fetal/neonatal platelets, not the autoimmune process causing the mother's own thrombocytopenia.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: ITP is an autoimmune, antibody-mediated process of platelet destruction (investigated via HLA/HPA antibody assays), mechanistically distinct from the alloimmune platelet destruction of NAIT (anti-HPA-1a against paternal antigen), the heparin/PF4-antibody-driven platelet activation of HIT, and the ADAMTS13-deficiency-driven microvascular consumption of TTP.

source_references.locator: Domain I Clinical Practice, High-Yield Facts (ITP line)
source_references.supports: ITP mechanism is platelet destruction; HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical for its investigation

source_references.locator: Domain I Clinical Practice, Major Concepts line
source_references.supports: ITP and HIT are named as distinct entities each with their own classification/pathogenesis, supporting HIT as a mechanistically separate distractor
HPA-1a is the primary alloantigen target in Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT), grounding the alloimmune distractor as mechanistically distinct from autoimmune ITP

source_references.locator: Domain IV Apheresis, High-Yield Facts (TTP line)
source_references.supports: TTP treatment (TPE) works by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors, grounding the ADAMTS13-deficiency distractor as a distinct consumptive mechanism
TTP's clinical pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, renal dysfunction) supports excluding TTP given this patient's normal smear and absence of other pentad features

Grep of all four files for 'pregnan' and 'gestational thrombocytopenia' near ITP/platelet content returns nothing relevant (only unrelated hits: HTLA/anti-D titration and cryoprecipitate fibrinogen targets in SRC-044). The item is effectively a generic 'ITP vs HIT vs TTP vs NAIT mechanism' questio

_audit.verdict_notes: Blueprint node is the pregnancy-specific sub-topic 'Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura in Pregnancy,' but none of the four approved sources contain any pregnancy-specific ITP content (no gestational thrombocytopenia differential, no pregnancy management thresholds, no neonatal/obstetric considerations). Grep of all four files for 'pregnan' and 'gestational thrombocytopenia' near ITP/platelet content returns nothing relevant (only unrelated hits: HTLA/anti-D titration and cryoprecipitate fibrinogen targets in SRC-044). The item is effectively a generic 'ITP vs HIT vs TTP vs NAIT mechanism' question with a pregnancy vignette wrapper -- none of the tested content is pregnancy-specific or sourced to pregnancy material, so it does not actually assess the assigned blueprint sub-node. The item author's own support_confidence is self-rated 'low,' consistent with this gap. On the narrower question of whether the keyed mechanism is correct: SRC-010 line 15 ('ITP involves platelet destruction where HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical for investigation') and line 7 (ITP and HIT listed as distinct entities with separate classification/pathogenesis) support option C as the best answer and support HIT (B) as a mechanistically distinct distractor. SRC-044 line 67 ('HPA-1a: Primary target in Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT)') grounds option D as the NAIT distractor. SRC-010 line 59 ('TPE is a primary treatment for TTP by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors') loosely grounds option A as the TTP distractor. However, none of these source lines actually specify the detailed mechanistic wording used in the options/rationales (e.g., 'IgG autoantibodies,' 'ultra-large vWF multimers,' 'platelet-reactive antibodies causing pathologic activation,' 'antigen absent from the mother's own platelets') -- these are standard textbook elaborations layered on top of thin, one-line source facts, not verifiable at that level of detail in the four approved sources. Independently, C is still the best-supported answer among the four options and matches the key, and no other option is defensible, so single_best_answer holds. But given the total absence of pregnancy-specific sourced content for a node explicitly named '...in Pregnancy,' this item should be flagged for revision or re-mapping to the general ITP node rather than approved as pregnancy-specific content.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 74-year-old man with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation is switched from warfarin to a direct oral factor Xa inhibitor for stroke prophylaxis. Unlike his prior warfarin regimen, the new medication does not require routine INR monitoring for dose adjustment. When the patient asks why, the hematology consultant explains that factor Xa inhibitors achieve their anticoagulant effect through a fundamentally different pathophysiologic mechanism than vitamin K antagonists.

options.text: Direct, reversible binding to and inhibition of circulating and clot-bound factor Xa, preventing prothrombinase-mediated conversion of prothrombin to thrombin
Provision of supraphysiologic activated factor VII to bypass a circulating inhibitor directed against a specific clotting factor
Impairment of hepatic synthesis of functional vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X, producing nonfunctional precursor (PIVKA) proteins
Chelation of ionized calcium to interrupt the calcium-dependent enzymatic steps of the coagulation cascade
Replacement of consumed coagulation factors and fibrinogen to correct an ongoing consumptive coagulopathy

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Factor Xa inhibitors act by directly and reversibly inhibiting factor Xa itself (free and clot-bound), blocking the prothrombinase complex from converting prothrombin to thrombin. This is fundamentally different from vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin, which act upstream in the liver: per the source material, vitamin K deficiency/antagonism leads to production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA proteins) rather than adequate functional factor levels. Because factor Xa inhibitors do not alter hepatic synthesis of clotting factors, the INR (which reflects factors II, VII, and X activity) is not a validated or routinely used monitoring tool for this drug class.

option_explanations.A: Correct - direct, reversible inhibition of factor Xa (free and clot-bound) blocks thrombin generation without altering hepatic synthesis of clotting factors; this is the defining mechanism that distinguishes the drug class from vitamin K antagonists.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - this describes the mechanism of vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin, which impair hepatic synthesis of functional Factors II, VII, IX, and X, producing nonfunctional PIVKA proteins. Attributing this synthesis-impairment mechanism to factor Xa inhibitors is a classic board trap that conflates the two oral anticoagulant classes.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect - this describes bypassing agents (e.g., recombinant factor VIIa, used alongside PCC) that manage high-titer Factor VIII inhibitors, not the mechanism of a factor Xa-inhibiting drug.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - this describes factor and fibrinogen replacement therapy (platelets, plasma, cryoprecipitate) used to manage the consumptive coagulopathy of DIC, not a pharmacologic anticoagulant's mechanism of action.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - this describes the mechanism of citrate, the anticoagulant used in blood collection and apheresis, which chelates ionized calcium and can cause citrate toxicity/hypocalcemia; it is unrelated to how factor Xa inhibitors act.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Direct factor Xa inhibitors act directly on already-formed factor Xa rather than impairing hepatic synthesis of vitamin K-dependent factors, which is why INR monitoring (validated for vitamin K antagonists) does not apply to this drug class.

source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency/antagonism causes production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, X (PIVKA proteins) via impaired hepatic synthesis - basis for correct-answer contrast and distractor B
Factor VIII inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents such as PCC or recombinant Factor VIIa - basis for distractor C
DIC is a consumption coagulopathy requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate - basis for distractor D

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Massive Transfusion Protocols, Citrate Toxicity
source_references.supports: Citrate anticoagulation chelates ionized calcium, producing hypocalcemia (citrate toxicity) - basis for distractor E

review.notes: None of the four approved sources mention factor Xa inhibitors, DOACs, rivaroxaban/apixaban/edoxaban, prothrombinase, or thrombin generation (confirmed via full read + exhaustive grep of all 4 files). The stem's clinical premise (DOAC vs warfarin, no INR monitoring needed) and the keyed answer's core mechanism (direct reversible Xa inhibition blocking prothrombinase-mediated thrombin generation) are entirely absent from the source set - the question's own source_references list tellingly cites support only for distractors B/C/D/E (all correctly grounded in SRC-002 Node 47: PIVKA proteins from

_audit.verdict_notes: None of the four approved sources mention factor Xa inhibitors, DOACs, rivaroxaban/apixaban/edoxaban, prothrombinase, or thrombin generation (confirmed via full read + exhaustive grep of all 4 files). The stem's clinical premise (DOAC vs warfarin, no INR monitoring needed) and the keyed answer's core mechanism (direct reversible Xa inhibition blocking prothrombinase-mediated thrombin generation) are entirely absent from the source set - the question's own source_references list tellingly cites support only for distractors B/C/D/E (all correctly grounded in SRC-002 Node 47: PIVKA proteins from vitamin K deficiency, Factor VIII inhibitor bypassing agents, DIC replacement therapy) and citrate hypocalcemia in SRC-032 Node 16, but offers zero citation for option A itself. Medically A is unambiguously the correct answer and I agree with it independently, but that correctness is imported from outside knowledge, not derived from these approved sources, so this item fails the source-grounding requirement for its central content despite well-sourced distractors.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 58-year-old woman with septic shock develops diffuse oozing from intravenous catheter sites and surgical incisions. Laboratory evaluation shows thrombocytopenia and a low fibrinogen level, and the clinical picture is consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). The transfusion medicine physician recommends replacement with platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's consumptive coagulopathy?

options.text: Decreased hepatic synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and von Willebrand factor
Production of nonfunctional vitamin K–dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA) from vitamin K deficiency
Simultaneous pathologic activation of both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems, consuming platelets, coagulation factors, and fibrinogen
Development of a high-titer autoantibody that neutralizes factor VIII activity
Impaired hepatic clearance of activated clotting factors from underlying liver dysfunction

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The source material defines DIC as a consumption coagulopathy in which both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically activated simultaneously. This dual-pathway activation consumes platelets, clotting factors, and fibrinogen, producing the bleeding diathesis seen in this patient and directly explaining why replacement therapy targets platelets (consumed), plasma (consumed clotting factors), and cryoprecipitate (consumed fibrinogen).

option_explanations.C: Correct — matches the source description of DIC as simultaneous procoagulant and fibrinolytic system activation causing consumption of platelets, factors, and fibrinogen, which is why all three products are replaced.
option_explanations.A: This describes the mechanism of bleeding in liver disease, not DIC; liver disease reduces synthesis of most factors but characteristically spares factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, a distinction that does not fit a consumptive process.
option_explanations.B: This describes vitamin K deficiency, which produces functionally inactive (PIVKA) vitamin K–dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X rather than a consumptive coagulopathy with thrombocytopenia and fibrinolytic activation.
option_explanations.D: This describes an acquired factor VIII inhibitor, which is managed with bypassing agents such as PCC or recombinant factor VIIa rather than platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement, and does not explain global consumption of platelets and fibrinogen.
option_explanations.E: Impaired clearance of activated factors is part of the liver disease mechanism in the source material, not the primary process driving DIC's combined procoagulant/fibrinolytic consumption and thrombocytopenia.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Study Note: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: DIC defined as consumption coagulopathy with pathologic activation of both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems, requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement (Subdomain Node 47).
Liver disease mechanism (decreased synthesis of factors except VIII/vWF, impaired clearance of activated factors) used to construct distractors B and E.
Vitamin K deficiency mechanism (nonfunctional PIVKA factors II, VII, IX, X) used to construct distractor C.
Factor VIII inhibitor management with bypassing agents used to construct distractor D.

_audit.verdict_notes: Blueprint mismatch (primary issue): the assigned node is S1.e.vi.6 'Antithrombotic Therapy > Fibrinolytic Agents,' which in board content means therapeutic thrombolytic drugs (tPA/alteplase, streptokinase, urokinase, tenecteplase) used to treat/prevent thrombosis. None of the four approved sources contain any content on pharmacologic fibrinolytic/thrombolytic agents (confirmed via targeted search - zero hits for tPA, streptokinase, urokinase, alteplase, plasminogen activator, etc. in any of the four files). The question instead tests DIC pathophysiology, sourced entirely from SRC-002 'Subdomain Node 47 - Coagulation Defects & Management,' which belongs under the sibling blueprint branch 'Bleeding from Congenital and Acquired Coagulation Defects,' not 'Antithrombotic Therapy.' A candidate studying this question would not learn anything about fibrinolytic-agent pharmacology, dosing, indications, or bleeding-risk management, which is presumably the intent of the assigned node. This is a significant content-alignment defect even though the DIC facts themselves are reasonably grounded. Fact-level check within SRC-002 Node 47 (lines 33-45 of advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md): (A) 'DIC...consumption coagulopathy where both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically active; requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate' - matches, though the stem's specific phrase 'consuming platelets, coagulation factors, and fibrinogen' is an inference from the replacement-product list rather than an explicit statement. (B) 'Liver Disease: decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors (except Factor VIII and vWF) and impaired clearance of activated factors' - matches. (C) 'Vitamin K Deficiency: nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, X (PIVKA)' - matches exactly. (D) Source only states 'Factor VIII Inhibitors: High-titer inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents like PCC or rFVIIa' - it never characterizes the inhibitor as an 'autoantibody'; this is added clinical knowledge not present in the cited source, a minor unsupported-claim. (E) 'impaired clearance of activated factors' under Liver Disease - matches. Independent answer: A is clearly the best-supported choice among the five for describing DIC's mechanism, and no other option is defensible for the septic-shock/thrombocytopenia/low-fibrinogen vignette, so single_best_answer=true and it agrees with the key. Net: the underlying facts used are mostly grounded in SRC-002, but the question is built from the wrong subdomain node relative to its assigned blueprint slot, and one distractor (D) adds an unsourced mechanistic detail.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 58-year-old man with relapsed acute myeloid leukemia and profound neutropenia has invasive pulmonary aspergillosis refractory to antifungal therapy. He receives a granulocyte transfusion collected by apheresis from his HLA-haploidentical brother; because of urgent need, the unit is infused without gamma irradiation. Nine days later he develops fever, a diffuse rash, watery diarrhea, and rising liver enzymes, followed by pancytopenia — a clinical picture consistent with transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD). Which of the following best explains the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism of this reaction?

options.text: Preformed recipient antibodies against Human Neutrophil Antigen (HNA) determinants on the donor granulocytes immediately destroy the transfused cells, triggering an acute systemic inflammatory reaction

Viable, immunocompetent donor T lymphocytes contained in the cellular granulocyte product engraft in the immunosuppressed, HLA-disparate recipient and mount a cytotoxic attack against host tissues, a process the host is unable to reject because the product was not irradiated

Recipient antibodies directed against HLA Class I antigens expressed on the transfused donor granulocytes agglutinate the cells within the pulmonary microvasculature, producing an acute hypoxemic reaction
An undetected relevant transfusion-transmitted infection (RTTI) in the donor, missed on standard infectious disease screening, was transmitted to the recipient via the cellular product

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: TA-GVHD occurs when a cellular blood product containing viable, immunocompetent donor lymphocytes is transfused into a host who cannot reject them (due to immunosuppression and/or HLA similarity, as with a haploidentical relative). The donor lymphocytes recognize the host's HLA antigens as foreign and attack host tissue (skin, gut, liver, marrow), producing the rash, diarrhea, transaminitis, and pancytopenia described. The source material establishes that irradiation of cellular blood components is the specific measure that prevents this mechanism, and that HLA is highly polymorphic and expressed on nucleated cells, providing the foreign-antigen target; haploidentical/related-donor settings are separately linked to GVHD-risk discussion in the sources.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the documented irradiation-preventable mechanism of TA-GVHD — viable donor lymphocytes attacking an immunocompromised, HLA-disparate host that cannot reject them.
option_explanations.C: Distractor: correctly uses the fact that HLA Class I is expressed on nucleated cells (including granulocytes), but describes an acute antibody-mediated pulmonary destruction reaction, not the delayed multisystem GVHD picture in this vignette.
option_explanations.D: Distractor: correctly invokes the RTTI screening concept, but an infectious transmission does not explain a biopsy-consistent GVHD pattern occurring 9 days after a product that underwent standard donor screening.
option_explanations.A: Distractor: correctly invokes HNA as a non-HLA leukocyte antigen determinant, but describes an immediate antibody-mediated destructive reaction rather than the delayed donor-lymphocyte engraftment-and-attack mechanism that defines TA-GVHD.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: TA-GVHD is the archetypal mechanism-based adverse event for cellular blood products such as granulocyte concentrates, which are richly cellular and cannot be leukoreduced without destroying the therapeutic granulocytes. Viable donor lymphocytes in the product engraft in an immunosuppressed or HLA-disparate (e.g., haploidentical-related-donor) recipient and attack host tissue, a reaction the host cannot reject; irradiation — not leukoreduction — is the preventive intervention.

source_references.supports: States that irradiation of standard cellular blood components (RBCs/platelets) prevents TA-GVHD, and that only HPC grafts are the exception to this rule — establishing irradiation as the mechanism-linked preventive measure.

source_references.locator: Domain 4 §1-2, MHC Organization and Non-HLA Determinants
source_references.supports: Establishes HLA Class I expression on most nucleated cells and extreme HLA polymorphism, and lists HNA/MICA as non-HLA determinants evaluated in leukocyte histocompatibility testing, used to construct the correct-answer rationale and the HNA distractor.
Notes that haploidentical/mismatched-sibling donors are specifically discussed in relation to GVHD risk/prevention, supporting the vignette's related-donor framing.
Defines RTTI (relevant transfusion-transmitted infection) and donor infectious-disease screening, used to construct the infectious-transmission distractor.

review.notes: None of the four cited sources contain the word 'granulocyte' anywhere (verified by grep across all four files) — the entire clinical vignette (apheresis granulocyte collection, haploidentical-brother granulocyte donor, AML with refractory invasive aspergillosis, unirradiated granulocyte unit, 9-day post-transfusion rash/diarrhea/transaminitis/pancytopenia) is invented relative to these sources, not merely thinly supported. The blueprint node is specifically 'Granulocyte Transfusion (Primary Donor Issues) > Adverse Events,' yet none of the sources discuss granulocyte transfusion as a topic at

_audit.verdict_notes: None of the four cited sources contain the word 'granulocyte' anywhere (verified by grep across all four files) — the entire clinical vignette (apheresis granulocyte collection, haploidentical-brother granulocyte donor, AML with refractory invasive aspergillosis, unirradiated granulocyte unit, 9-day post-transfusion rash/diarrhea/transaminitis/pancytopenia) is invented relative to these sources, not merely thinly supported. The blueprint node is specifically 'Granulocyte Transfusion (Primary Donor Issues) > Adverse Events,' yet none of the sources discuss granulocyte transfusion as a topic at all. The only TA-GVHD-relevant sentence in any source is one line in SRC-040 (line 129, under Subdomain Node 24 'HPC Processing & Infusion'): 'While the recipient needs irradiated standard blood components (RBCs/Platelets) to prevent TA-GVHD, the HPC graft itself must not be irradiated.' That single sentence establishes only that irradiation of standard cellular components prevents TA-GVHD — it does NOT state the mechanism asserted in option A (viable donor T-lymphocyte engraftment, cytotoxic attack on HLA-disparate host tissue, host inability to reject). Critically, the source document itself is truncated exactly where the real TA-GVHD content would begin: line 143 reads 'Status Update: Subdomain Nodes 23 and 24 are complete. Node 25 (TA-GVHD) is next. Ready to proceed?' — the TA-GVHD node was never actually written into this source file, so the explanation's detailed mechanism is drawn from outside general medical knowledge, not from the approved source. The question's own citation of the 'Haploidentical Success' pearl (SRC-040, Node 24) to support 'the vignette's related-donor framing' is a misapplied citation: that pearl is specifically about post-transplant-cyclophosphamide enabling haploidentical HPC (stem cell) transplant donors, an entirely different clinical context (engraftment for hematopoietic reconstitution) from granulocyte transfusion (temporary neutrophil support), and conflating the two overstates source support. The HLA Class I citation (SRC-028) supports 'most nucleated cells and platelets' express HLA Class I but does not specifically mention granulocytes, and the HNA/MICA citation is a bare terminology listing with no mechanistic description matching option D's antibody-mediated destruction narrative. The RTTI citation (SRC-003) is a generic definition unconnected to this vignette's 9-day GVHD-consistent picture. Despite this near-total lack of grounding in the provided sources, option A is independently the medically correct textbook description of TA-GVHD pathophysiology (viable immunocompetent donor lymphocytes attacking an immunosuppressed/HLA-disparate host that cannot reject them), so if judged purely on general hematology/transfusion-medicine correctness there is a single best answer and it matches the key. The author's own 'support_confidence: low' tag and 'confidence: low/moderate' tags on the source_references are an implicit admission of this weak grounding, but given that the central mechanism claim and the entire granulocyte-transfusion scenario are absent from the four approved sources, this should be scored as a source-support failure rather than merely low confidence.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 34-year-old woman with congenital antithrombin deficiency is scheduled for elective orthopedic surgery. To cover the perioperative period, her transfusion medicine physician selects a recombinant antithrombin (rhAT) concentrate rather than a plasma-derived antithrombin concentrate manufactured from large pools of donor plasma. What is the fundamental mechanistic basis for the improved viral-transmission safety profile of the recombinant product compared with the plasma-derived concentrate?

options.text: The recombinant protein is expressed using DNA technology in a non-human production system (e.g., transgenic animal milk or cell culture) rather than being purified from pooled human plasma, so no human-origin donor material enters the manufacturing process
The recombinant product's improved safety is explained by its shorter circulating half-life compared with plasma-derived factor concentrates, which limits cumulative exposure to any contaminant
The recombinant protein is produced by attenuating a viral vector that carries the antithrombin gene, which removes the vector's pathogenicity while preserving protein expression
The recombinant protein undergoes the same cold-fractionation and pooled-plasma viral inactivation steps used for plasma derivatives, which is why its safety profile is equivalent

The recombinant protein is purified from a small pool of extensively screened human donors rather than the large pools used for standard plasma derivatives, which reduces but does not eliminate viral exposure

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Recombinant proteins such as rhAT are manufactured using DNA technology in non-human expression systems (e.g., transgenic goat milk or cell culture) rather than being fractionated from pooled human donor plasma. Because the manufacturing process never draws on a human plasma source, recombinant products carry no risk of transmitting a human-origin virus, in contrast to plasma derivatives, which require rigorous viral inactivation/pathogen reduction precisely because they originate from pooled human plasma.

option_explanations.A: Correct: DNA-technology production in a non-human host (transgenic animal milk or cell culture) is the mechanistic reason recombinant products carry no human-origin viral transmission risk.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes the plasma-derivative pathway. Plasma derivatives require fractionation and viral inactivation because they are pooled from human donors; recombinant products are not produced this way, so this option misattributes the plasma-derivative process to the recombinant product.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect and a classic board trap: it assumes recombinant products still originate from human donors (just a smaller, more screened pool). In fact, recombinant technology bypasses human donor plasma entirely by using DNA technology in a non-human expression system.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: half-life is a pharmacokinetic property unrelated to viral safety, and the short-half-life concern in the source material applies to factors VII, VIII, and IX (which require frequent dosing), not to the viral-transmission mechanism of antithrombin production.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source describes recombinant protein manufacture as occurring in a non-human expression system (transgenic animal milk or cell culture), not via an attenuated viral vector carrying the gene into a production host; no viral vector is invoked in the described mechanism.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: None of the four approved sources address non-viral gene therapy delivery mechanisms (e.g., lipid nanoparticles, naked plasmid DNA, electroporation-based ex vivo gene editing). The only DNA/biotechnology-related content available is recombinant protein manufacturing technology (SRC-051, Subdomain Node 39), which is a related but distinct concept: it describes producing a therapeutic protein in a non-human expression system for administration to the patient, not the introduction of genetic material into a patient's own cells. This question is the closest legitimately source-grounded proxy for the assigned blueprint angle and is marked low-confidence accordingly; a fully on-topic non-viral gene therapy question would require sources this set does not contain.

source_references.supports: Recombinant proteins are produced via DNA technology (e.g., transgenic goat milk or cell cultures) to provide targeted therapy without the risk of human-origin viral transmission; rhAT is indicated for congenital antithrombin deficiency in surgical patients and generally lacks antibody-formation risk; plasma derivatives are manufactured from pooled human plasma via fractionation and undergo viral

review.notes: All asserted facts (plasma-derivative pooling/viral inactivation, recombinant DNA production in transgenic goat milk/cell culture avoiding human-origin viral risk, rhAT indication, factor VII/VIII/IX short half-life as unrelated PK property) are accurately grounded in SRC-051 Subdomain Node 39. Option A is unambiguously the best-supported answer and matches the key; B/C/D/E are correctly refuted by source content. However, the question does not actually test the assigned blueprint node 'Non-Viral Gene Therapy' (S2.d.vi) — it tests recombinant therapeutic protein manufacturing (ex vivo producti

_audit.verdict_notes: All asserted facts (plasma-derivative pooling/viral inactivation, recombinant DNA production in transgenic goat milk/cell culture avoiding human-origin viral risk, rhAT indication, factor VII/VIII/IX short half-life as unrelated PK property) are accurately grounded in SRC-051 Subdomain Node 39. Option A is unambiguously the best-supported answer and matches the key; B/C/D/E are correctly refuted by source content. However, the question does not actually test the assigned blueprint node 'Non-Viral Gene Therapy' (S2.d.vi) — it tests recombinant therapeutic protein manufacturing (ex vivo production of a protein for infusion), a distinct concept from somatic gene therapy (introducing genetic material into a patient's own cells via viral or non-viral vectors: lipid nanoparticles, naked plasmid DNA, electroporation, etc.). I independently confirmed via full-text search that none of the four approved sources contain any content on gene therapy delivery mechanisms at all. The question's own teaching_point self-discloses this as a low-confidence proxy substitution due to a genuine source gap. This is a legitimate, severe blueprint-mismatch: a learner studying this node would not learn anything about non-viral gene therapy from this item despite it being filed under that topic.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 52-year-old woman with warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (Hgb 4.8 g/dL, brisk reticulocytosis, spherocytes on peripheral smear, and a direct antiglobulin test positive for IgG) continues to hemolyze despite high-dose corticosteroids. While rituximab is being initiated, the transfusion medicine service starts therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) as a temporizing measure. Which of the following best explains the mechanistic rationale for using TPE in this setting and why its benefit is expected to be transient?

options.text: TPE removes complement (C3) fragments that are already bound to the red cell surface, thereby preventing further extravascular splenic clearance

TPE mechanically removes circulating pathogenic autoantibody from the plasma compartment, producing a transient reduction in antibody available to bind red cells; because antibody-producing plasma cells are unaffected by the procedure, the benefit is short-lived unless paired with immunosuppressive therapy
TPE selectively removes IgG-coated red blood cells from the circulation using the plasma separator, correcting the anemia by eliminating the antibody-coated cells themselves
TPE restores a deficient plasma protease that is responsible for the ongoing red cell destruction in this patient
TPE produces durable remission by eliminating the autoreactive antibody-producing cell clones responsible for the hemolysis

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: AIHA is driven by autoantibody that binds red cell antigens and shortens cell survival. TPE works by the same general mechanical principle used in TTP (removing a pathogenic circulating substance from plasma): it physically removes plasma, lowering the circulating autoantibody available to opsonize red cells. Because the procedure does not affect the antibody-producing plasma cells, autoantibody titers rebound once exchange stops, making TPE a temporizing bridge that must be paired with therapy aimed at antibody production (e.g., corticosteroids, rituximab, as listed for WAIHA) for durable control.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect: TPE separates and removes plasma, not cellular elements. The sources describe red cell exchange as the distinct apheresis modality that removes red cells directly (e.g., for sickle cell disease); this option confuses TPE with red cell exchange.
option_explanations.B: Correct: TPE physically depletes circulating autoantibody from plasma, but plasma cells that produce the antibody are untouched, so the effect wanes unless combined with immunosuppression such as corticosteroids or rituximab (the therapies listed for WAIHA).
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes the TTP mechanism, where TPE with FFP replacement corrects an ADAMTS13 deficiency/inhibitor. It is a classic board trap that conflates TTP pathophysiology (missing plasma protease) with AIHA, which is instead caused by autoantibody-mediated red cell destruction, not a protease deficiency.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: TPE removes soluble plasma constituents, not proteins already fixed to the red cell membrane. Complement- and antibody-coated cells continue to be cleared extravascularly by splenic macrophages regardless of the current plasma antibody level.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: eliminating autoreactive antibody-producing cells is the goal of immunosuppressive/targeted therapy (e.g., rituximab, corticosteroids), which the sources list as WAIHA treatments, not the goal of TPE, which is a mechanical, temporary process that does not act on antibody-producing cells.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: TPE in antibody-mediated hemolysis acts by mechanically depleting circulating pathogenic antibody from plasma, not by destroying antibody-producing cells or removing antibody/complement already fixed to red cells; its benefit is therefore transient and it must be paired with immunosuppression, distinguishing it mechanistically from red cell exchange and from TPE's role in TTP (replacing a deficient plasma protease).

source_references.locator: Definitions; Clinical Presentations; Management; High-Yield Comparison Points sections
source_references.supports: AIHA pathophysiology (autoantibody against self RBC antigens causing shortened survival); WAIHA is IgG-mediated and predominantly extravascular/splenic; standard WAIHA management list is corticosteroids, IVIG, splenectomy, rituximab.

General mechanistic principle of TPE (illustrated via TTP: TPE removes a pathogenic circulating substance, the ADAMTS13 inhibitor, from plasma) and that red cell exchange is a distinct apheresis modality from TPE that removes cells rather than plasma.

review.notes: The question's entire clinical premise — that TPE is used as a temporizing bridge therapy in corticosteroid-refractory WAIHA while rituximab takes effect, and the specific mechanistic explanation for why (autoantibody depletion from plasma without affecting antibody-producing plasma cells) — is never stated in any of the four sources. Exhaustive search of SRC-010, SRC-019, SRC-027, and SRC-040 confirms: (1) SRC-019's WAIHA management list is explicitly 'Corticosteroids (first-line), IVIG, splenectomy, or rituximab' — TPE is NOT listed as a WAIHA therapy anywhere. (2) SRC-010's Apheresis domain

_audit.verdict_notes: The question's entire clinical premise — that TPE is used as a temporizing bridge therapy in corticosteroid-refractory WAIHA while rituximab takes effect, and the specific mechanistic explanation for why (autoantibody depletion from plasma without affecting antibody-producing plasma cells) — is never stated in any of the four sources. Exhaustive search of SRC-010, SRC-019, SRC-027, and SRC-040 confirms: (1) SRC-019's WAIHA management list is explicitly 'Corticosteroids (first-line), IVIG, splenectomy, or rituximab' — TPE is NOT listed as a WAIHA therapy anywhere. (2) SRC-010's Apheresis domain states only 'TPE is a primary treatment for TTP... by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors' and lists Red Cell Exchange for Sickle Cell Disease — TPE is never linked to AIHA. (3) SRC-040 (the source most focused on immune hemolysis) explicitly names a different apheresis-adjacent bridge therapy for refractory AIHA — 'Eculizumab: This complement inhibitor has been successfully used as a bridge to therapy for patients with acute, brisk hemolysis in some types of AIHA' — but says nothing about TPE for AIHA. (4) SRC-027 only lists 'Therapeutic Apheresis Indications (Ch 25)' as a chapter heading with no AIHA-specific content, and flags AIHA treatment detail as a 'Thin Area' with 'less granular therapeutic detail.' The question's own source_references tacitly admit this: SRC-010 is cited as supporting only the 'general mechanistic principle of TPE (illustrated via TTP)' — i.e., the writer extrapolated the TTP mechanism to AIHA by analogy rather than finding it stated for AIHA. This is a real-world accurate clinical fact (TPE genuinely is used adjunctively in severe refractory AIHA in actual practice) but it is not grounded in these four approved sources, so it fails the 'every asserted fact must be supported' bar. Independently, among the five answer choices as written, B is still the best/most medically defensible option (A confuses TPE with RBC exchange; C is the TTP/ADAMTS13 trap; D and E are both refuted by basic apheresis physiology), so the key is not actually wrong — but the stem's premise and the correct-answer rationale are synthesized from outside general medical knowledge rather than from the cited board-prep sources, which is exactly the kind of gap this audit is meant to catch given the sources' own 'Thin Area' admission about AIHA treatment granularity.

questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A 52-year-old man is 3 weeks post-orthotopic liver transplant and maintained on tacrolimus and mycophenolate mofetil. Ten days after receiving a unit of non-irradiated RBCs for postoperative anemia, he develops fever, a diffuse maculopapular rash, watery diarrhea, and rising transaminases. A skin biopsy shows a lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction. Which of the following best explains the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism of this presentation?

options.text: Donor-origin lymphocytes carried in the transfused unit are producing antibodies directed against the recipient's own red cell antigens, causing hemolysis
A drug the patient is taking has formed a hapten-RBC membrane complex that is inducing antibody-mediated destruction of the recipient's own red cells

Viable donor T-lymphocytes within the transfused, non-irradiated cellular component were not inactivated; they engrafted, proliferated, and are now mounting a direct attack against the recipient's skin, gut, and liver
Persisting recipient isoagglutinins are lysing developing donor erythroid precursors in the marrow
Excessive donor red cells within a major ABO-mismatched cellular graft triggered recipient isoagglutinin-mediated hemolysis at the time of infusion

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The described multisystem picture (rash, fever, diarrhea, liver dysfunction with a lymphocytic tissue infiltrate) matches transfusion-associated GVHD, which the sources define as caused by donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues. The sources further indicate that irradiation (or pathogen reduction) of cellular blood components is required specifically to 'inactivate contaminating T-cells,' implying that if this step is omitted, viable donor T-lymphocytes in the component can survive, engraft, and attack the recipient — the mechanism underlying this patient's presentation.

option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the sourced definition of GVHD as a multi-system syndrome (rash, fever, diarrhea, liver dysfunction) caused by donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues, and matches the rationale given for why cellular components must be irradiated ('to inactivate contaminating T-cells').
option_explanations.A: This describes Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome, in which donor-origin lymphocytes in a graft produce antibodies against the recipient's RBC antigens causing hemolysis — an antibody-mediated process typically discussed after ABO-mismatched HSCT, not a T-cell attack on skin/gut/liver, and it does not explain rash, diarrhea, or hepatic dysfunction as a multisystem process.
option_explanations.D: This describes Pure Red Cell Aplasia, where recipient antibodies persist and lyse developing donor erythroid precursors after major ABO-mismatched HSCT. This is a recipient-versus-graft (not graft-versus-host) phenomenon presenting as isolated erythroid failure (reticulocytes <1% for >60 days), not the rash/fever/diarrhea/liver picture described.
option_explanations.E: The sources describe acute hemolysis occurring during graft infusion in major ABO mismatches when the graft contains excessive donor RBCs — an antibody/complement-mediated hemolytic event occurring at the time of infusion, not a delayed T-cell-mediated multisystem attack on recipient tissue.
option_explanations.B: This describes the hapten mechanism of drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia, in which a drug bound to the RBC membrane induces antibody-mediated hemolysis. This is unrelated to donor T-cell activity and does not account for the rash, diarrhea, and liver dysfunction seen here.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: TA-GVHD arises when viable, immunocompetent donor T-lymphocytes in a non-irradiated cellular blood component are not inactivated, allowing them to engraft, proliferate, and attack the immunocompromised recipient's skin, gut, liver, and marrow; irradiation/pathogen reduction of components prevents this by inactivating the contaminating T-cells. This mechanism (graft-versus-host, T-cell-mediated tissue attack) must be distinguished from superficially similar donor-lymphocyte phenomena such as Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome (antibody-mediated hemolysis) and Pure Red Cell Aplasia (recipient-versus-graft erythroid suppression). Note: the approved sources establish this general TA-GVHD mechanism and prevention strategy in the context of HSCT/immunocompromised recipients but do not explicitly discuss solid organ transplant recipients as an at-risk population for TA-GVHD, so this specific population linkage is extrapolated rather than directly sourced.

source_references.supports: Defines GVHD as a multi-system syndrome (rash, fever, diarrhea, liver dysfunction) caused by donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues.
States cellular blood components must be irradiated/pathogen-reduced to inactivate contaminating T-cells, implying the mechanism of TA-GVHD is uninactivated donor T-cells surviving in the component.
Parallel statement that HSCT recipients require irradiated/pathogen-reduced components for life to prevent GVHD from donor T-cells.

source_references.locator: Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome definition and PRCA definition/pearls
source_references.supports: Supports distractor B (PLS: donor lymphocytes produce antibody against recipient RBC antigens) and distractor C (PRCA: recipient antibodies lyse donor erythroid precursors).
Supports distractor D: acute hemolysis during graft infusion in major mismatches with excessive donor RBCs.
Supports distractor E: hapten-type drug-induced immune hemolysis mechanism.

review.notes: Verified against all four sources directly. The general TA-GVHD mechanism/definition is well grounded: SRC-035 line 31 states verbatim 'GVHD: A multi-system syndrome (rash, fever, diarrhea, liver dysfunction) caused by donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues,' and line 61 states cellular components 'must be irradiated or pathogen-reduced for life to inactivate contaminating T-cells' (paralleled in SRC-038 line 61 and SRC-028 line 129). Distractors B (PLS) and C (PRCA) are strongly sourced verbatim in SRC-035 lines 19-21/87 and SRC-028 line 93. Distractor D (acute hemolysis from excessive don

_audit.verdict_notes: Verified against all four sources directly. The general TA-GVHD mechanism/definition is well grounded: SRC-035 line 31 states verbatim 'GVHD: A multi-system syndrome (rash, fever, diarrhea, liver dysfunction) caused by donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues,' and line 61 states cellular components 'must be irradiated or pathogen-reduced for life to inactivate contaminating T-cells' (paralleled in SRC-038 line 61 and SRC-028 line 129). Distractors B (PLS) and C (PRCA) are strongly sourced verbatim in SRC-035 lines 19-21/87 and SRC-028 line 93. Distractor D (acute hemolysis from excessive donor RBCs in major-mismatch graft infusion) matches SRC-035 line 25. Distractor E (Cefotetan/Penicillin hapten-type DIIHA) matches SRC-040 lines 21, 37 closely. HOWEVER, the blueprint node under test is specifically 'Patient Populations at Risk - Solid Organ Transplants' for TA-GVHD, and I confirmed via targeted searches (irradiat*, solid organ, immunocompromis*, at risk, congenital, neonat*, directed donation, HLA-matched, Hodgkin) that NONE of the four sources ever state or imply that solid organ transplant recipients are an at-risk population for TA-GVHD or require irradiated components. The only 'solid organ transplant' mentions in the sources (SRC-035 line 5, SRC-038 lines 9/73, SRC-028 line 45) are unrelated contrasts about ABO-matching rules for HSCT vs. solid organ transplant, not about TA-GVHD risk. All TA-GVHD/irradiation-necessity statements in the sources are scoped explicitly to HSCT recipients. The entire clinical premise of the vignette (a liver-transplant patient being a TA-GVHD risk case) is therefore an unsourced extrapolation onto the exact blueprint node being tested -- an issue the question's own teaching point/explanation candidly admits ('the approved sources ... do not explicitly discuss solid organ transplant recipients as an at-risk population ... extrapolated rather than directly sourced'). Additionally, the stem's specific clinical detail 'skin biopsy shows a lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction' is medically accurate for GVHD but is nowhere in these four sources -- an invented histologic detail. Despite these grounding problems, among the five answer options as written, A remains clearly the single best/most defensible choice matching the sourced GVHD definition, and B-E are each clearly sourced as distinct, non-matching phenomena, so there is no genuine answer-choice ambiguity. The core problem is that the blueprint-specific premise (solid organ transplant as an at-risk population) lacks source grounding, not that the keyed answer is wrong among the given choices.

questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A 68-year-old man with transfusion-dependent myelodysplastic syndrome has received more than 40 RBC units over the past 18 months on a fixed 2-3 week transfusion interval. He has no history of gastrointestinal bleeding, and his renal function and hepatic synthetic function remain normal. He now presents with new-onset diabetes mellitus and a restrictive cardiomyopathy. Which of the following best explains the underlying physiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's iron accumulation and resulting organ dysfunction?

options.text: The additive solution used to preserve and store the transfused RBC units supplies supplemental iron that is absorbed directly into the recipient's circulation with each transfusion

His iron accumulation is primarily driven by compensatory upregulation of intestinal dietary iron absorption from chronic anemia, rather than from the iron content of the transfused RBC units themselves

The human body has no regulated physiologic pathway for excreting excess iron, so the iron content of every transfused RBC unit accumulates cumulatively, first saturating reticuloendothelial macrophage storage before depositing in parenchymal organs such as the heart, liver, and endocrine glands

Reticuloendothelial macrophages have an essentially unlimited capacity to sequester excess iron, so once iron is taken up by macrophages after senescent RBC breakdown it cannot redistribute into parenchymal tissue

Because his transfusions are already given on a defined clinical interval rather than as liberal 2-unit orders, using a restrictive single-unit transfusion strategy would not have reduced his cumulative iron exposure, since the same total iron mass is eventually transfused regardless of ordering strategy

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Iron homeostasis in humans is regulated almost entirely at the level of intestinal absorption; there is no significant active excretory pathway for iron. Each transfused RBC unit delivers hemoglobin-bound iron that, once the cells senesce, is processed and stored by reticuloendothelial macrophages. With chronic, repeated transfusion this iron load exceeds RES storage capacity and iron progressively redistributes into parenchymal cells (hepatocytes, cardiomyocytes, endocrine tissue), producing the organ dysfunction (diabetes, cardiomyopathy) described. Note: this core iron-metabolism mechanism is standard transfusion-medicine physiology and is not itself detailed in the four approved sources reviewed, which is why this question's support_confidence is rated low; the sources were used only to ground the surrounding distractor logic.

option_explanations.C: Correct. Absence of a regulated excretory pathway for iron, combined with ongoing transfusional iron delivery, explains progressive RES saturation and eventual parenchymal (cardiac/hepatic/endocrine) iron deposition — this is the foundational mechanism underlying transfusion-induced iron overload.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. SRC-032 describes RBC additive solutions as being used to adjust final hematocrit (55%-65% with additive solution vs up to 80% without) and extend shelf life during storage — not as a source of supplemental iron delivered to the recipient. The iron burden comes from the hemoglobin within the transfused red cells themselves, not from the storage/preservative solution.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. SRC-032's Patient Blood Management content specifically supports restrictive, single-unit-based ordering ("why give 2 when 1 will do") as reducing the total number of units a patient receives compared with liberal 2-unit ordering; over a chronic transfusion course this reduces (or slows) cumulative iron exposure rather than being neutral to it, contradicting this option's claim.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect distractor. In a transfusion-dependent patient with an intact, non-bleeding GI tract, the transfused RBC units — not increased dietary absorption — are the dominant source of the iron load; this option misattributes the mechanism.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Reticuloendothelial macrophage iron storage capacity is finite, not unlimited; once exceeded, iron redistributes to parenchymal organs, which is precisely how the endocrine and cardiac dysfunction in this vignette arise. If RES storage were truly unlimited and non-redistributive, transfusional organ iron toxicity would not occur.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Because humans lack a regulated iron-excretion mechanism, each unit of transfused RBCs contributes iron that cannot be actively eliminated; with chronic transfusion this iron saturates reticuloendothelial storage and redistributes to parenchymal organs (liver, heart, endocrine glands), which is the pathophysiologic rationale for both minimizing unnecessary transfusion exposure (PBM) and for iron chelation therapy in transfusion-dependent patients.

source_references.supports: PBM section describes restrictive vs liberal transfusion thresholds and the 'why give 2 when 1 will do' single-unit ordering principle, used to ground why option C's claim (that ordering strategy doesn't affect cumulative iron dose) is incorrect.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 19 - Blood Component Manufacturing & QC, RBC Component Requirements
source_references.supports: RBC Component Requirements section describes additive solution (AS) use for adjusting hematocrit during storage, supporting that AS functions in volume/Hct management rather than as an iron-delivery vehicle, used to refute option B.

The ONLY hit for 'iron' across all four documents is a single unrelated line in SRC-032 about preoperative anemia management ('iron, EPO... before elective surgery') - nothing on transfusion-induced iron overload, RES iron handling, parenchymal redistribution, or chelation goals.
