_audit.verdict_notes: Independently grepped all four approved sources for iron/iron overload/chelation/hemosiderosis/reticuloendothelial content. Result: zero coverage. The ONLY hit for 'iron' across all four documents is a single unrelated line in SRC-032 about preoperative anemia management ('iron, EPO... before elective surgery') - nothing on transfusion-induced iron overload, RES iron handling, parenchymal redistribution, or chelation goals. The question's own explanation admits this: 'this core iron-metabolism mechanism... is not itself detailed in the four approved sources reviewed.' That is a direct violation of the source-grounding requirement - the entire physiologic rationale for the keyed answer (A) is asserted from outside knowledge, not from SRC-003, SRC-032, SRC-027, or SRC-028. Only the peripheral distractor logic is actually sourced: SRC-032 (~line 143) supports option B's rationale (additive solution adjusts Hct 55-65% vs <=80% without additive, not an iron-delivery vehicle) and SRC-032 (~lines 55-71) supports option C's rationale (restrictive/single-unit 'why give 2 when 1 will do' PBM strategy reduces cumulative units, contradicting C's claim of no effect). But B/C support alone cannot rescue the item: the keyed answer's central assertion is unsourced. Additionally this appears to be a blueprint mismatch: node S6.d.v.1 is 'Management > Goals' under Transfusion-Induced Iron Overload (i.e., goals of chelation/monitoring, targets to prevent organ damage), but the question instead tests basic mechanistic pathophysiology (why iron accumulates) in a patient who already has established organ damage, not management goals or targets. Independently, among the five options, A is the medically correct/uniquely defensible answer per standard hematology teaching (no regulated excretory pathway for iron; RES storage capacity is finite and iron redistributes to parenchymal organs once exceeded), so single_best_answer=true and answer_agrees=true on medical-correctness grounds - but this correctness cannot be verified against the approved source set, which simply does not discuss the topic. This item should not pass as source-grounded content; either better sources covering iron overload physiology need to be added to the approved set, or the item should be retargeted/rewritten to test content actually present in SRC-003/032/027/028, or moved out of the blueprint node it claims to cover (Management > Goals) into general pathophysiology if kept.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 29-year-old man has donated source plasma by serial automated plasmapheresis (with his red cells returned to him) up to twice weekly for the past 18 months through an FDA-regulated frequent-collection program. His collection facility recognizes that certain blood constituents can become depleted with very frequent component donation — the same concern that leads programs to monitor ferritin and counsel iron supplementation in frequent whole-blood donors — so his periodic donor-health screening also tracks his plasma protein levels for a downward trend. Which of the following best explains the underlying physiologic mechanism by which very frequent plasma component donation can lead to a decline in a donor's plasma protein levels over time?

options.text: A decline in plasma protein indicates a reactive infectious-disease marker that must be resolved through the donor reentry process before further donation is permitted
The hemoglobin/hematocrit eligibility thresholds checked at each donation visit are specifically designed to detect and prevent depletion of total plasma protein
The rate of plasma protein removal by serial apheresis collection outpaces the donor's synthetic capacity to regenerate the removed proteins between collections

The standard 8-week inter-donation interval required for whole-blood donors also governs source-plasma collection frequency, so any protein decline reflects a regulatory failure rather than donor physiology
Repeated venipuncture directly damages hepatocytes at the injection site, impairing the liver's ability to synthesize plasma proteins

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The approved sources establish that frequent donation can deplete a blood constituent when the collection-driven removal rate exceeds the body's capacity to regenerate that constituent between donations — explicitly documented for iron, where frequent whole-blood donation produces nonanemic iron deficiency and prompts ferritin monitoring and supplementation. Applying this same removal-versus-regeneration kinetic principle to serial plasma component collection, very frequent plasmapheresis can outpace the donor's synthetic replacement of plasma proteins, producing a measurable decline that donor-health monitoring is designed to catch, paralleling the iron-monitoring logic in the sources.

option_explanations.C: Correct: mirrors the source-documented mechanism for iron (donation-driven removal exceeding replacement/regenerative capacity), generalized here to plasma protein kinetics in a high-frequency plasma donor.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources state the 8-week interval applies to whole blood (with a separate 16-week interval for 2-unit RBC apheresis); they do not state this interval governs source-plasma collection frequency, so this option misapplies an unrelated regulatory rule and mischaracterizes depletion as a 'regulatory failure' rather than a physiologic supply/demand mismatch.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources list hemoglobin/hematocrit as physical examination eligibility thresholds (e.g., Hgb ≥13.0 g/dL male, ≥12.5 g/dL female) tied to anemia screening, not as a mechanism or safeguard targeting total plasma protein specifically.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources' reentry logic concerns donors deferred for reactive infectious markers (e.g., HBV, HCV/HIV, WNV/Zika, syphilis NAT/serology) regaining eligibility after confirmatory testing — it has no connection to plasma protein levels or depletion mechanisms.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: no source describes venipuncture causing direct hepatocyte injury; this option proposes an anatomically and physiologically implausible mechanism unsupported by any donor-health content in the sources.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Long-term physiologic effects of frequent donation follow a common kinetic principle: when donation-driven removal of a blood constituent outpaces the body's capacity to regenerate it, depletion results — a concept the sources document concretely for iron in frequent whole-blood donors and that generalizes conceptually to other constituents removed by high-frequency component collection.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 50 – Donor Health & Reentry Logic: Iron Store Mitigation; Infectious Reentry Logic
source_references.supports: Provides the only source-documented 'long-term effects of frequent donation' pathophysiology (nonanemic iron deficiency from donation-driven removal exceeding replacement, with ferritin monitoring/supplementation), used here as the grounded analog generalized to plasma protein depletion since no source directly addresses plasma proteins.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Study Note: Subdomain Node 30 – Donor Selection, Deferrals, and Physicals
source_references.supports: Supplies the donation-interval facts (8 weeks whole blood; 16 weeks for 2-unit RBC apheresis) and physical exam hemoglobin/hematocrit thresholds used to construct distractors B and C as misapplications of unrelated donor-eligibility rules.

review.notes: The word 'protein' does not appear anywhere in any of the four approved sources (SRC-002, SRC-003, SRC-004, SRC-011) — verified by exhaustive grep across all four files. None of the sources discuss plasma protein levels in donors, total protein testing/eligibility for source-plasma donors, or any mechanism by which frequent plasma component donation depletes plasma proteins. The only 'long-term effects of frequent donation' content in the entire source set is SRC-002's iron/ferritin passage (Subdomain Node 50: 'Frequent donation leads to nonanemic iron deficiency... ferritin testing... iron su

_audit.verdict_notes: The word 'protein' does not appear anywhere in any of the four approved sources (SRC-002, SRC-003, SRC-004, SRC-011) — verified by exhaustive grep across all four files. None of the sources discuss plasma protein levels in donors, total protein testing/eligibility for source-plasma donors, or any mechanism by which frequent plasma component donation depletes plasma proteins. The only 'long-term effects of frequent donation' content in the entire source set is SRC-002's iron/ferritin passage (Subdomain Node 50: 'Frequent donation leads to nonanemic iron deficiency... ferritin testing... iron supplementation'), which concerns a different constituent (iron) in a different donation modality (frequent whole-blood donation), not plasma proteins in source-plasma apheresis. The question's own explanation and source_references concede this: it explicitly states 'no source directly addresses plasma proteins' and marks confidence 'low', i.e., the writer is admitting the core physiologic claim underpinning the stem and the keyed answer is an analogy/extrapolation invented for this question rather than a fact stated in the approved sources. This fails the fully-grounded bar for source support — the central testable content of the blueprint node (S9.a.iv.3.c, Plasma Proteins) has zero coverage in these four sources, so the question should not have been built as source-grounded content. On the other hand, distractors B, C, D, E are each verifiably wrong against sourced facts: B misapplies the 8-week whole-blood/16-week 2-unit-RBC-apheresis interval (SRC-003, 'Subdomain Node 30') to source-plasma collection, which the source does not state; C misrepresents the Hgb (>=13.0 g/dL male / >=12.5 g/dL female) / Hct physical-exam thresholds (SRC-003) as a total-protein safeguard, which they are not; D misapplies the infectious-marker reentry logic (SRC-002, 'Subdomain Node 50') to protein decline, an unrelated topic; E's hepatocyte-injury-by-venipuncture mechanism appears nowhere in any source and is medically implausible. So among the five options as written, A is the only non-contradicted, medically coherent choice and is correct as a matter of real-world transfusion medicine (FDA does require total-protein monitoring in frequent source-plasma donors), making it the best answer by elimination and matching the key. But because the specific fact chain needed to justify A as 'the sources establish this' is not actually present in the source text — it is manufactured by analogy — this item fails strict source-grounding and should be reworked or dropped for this blueprint node given the sources currently available.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 45-year-old man with a large body-surface-area thermal burn is undergoing initial fluid resuscitation. Over the following hours he develops diffuse oozing from intravenous catheter sites and burn wound margins. Laboratory evaluation shows a prolonged PT and PTT, a falling fibrinogen level, and a falling platelet count. Which of the following best explains the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's bleeding diathesis?

options.text: An anamnestic antibody response directed against a red cell antigen to which the patient was previously sensitized
Simultaneous pathologic activation of the procoagulant and fibrinolytic cascades, producing a consumption coagulopathy that depletes platelets and clotting factors
Decreased hepatic synthesis of vitamin K–dependent clotting factors, producing nonfunctional PIVKA proteins
An antibody-mediated hypersensitivity reaction directed against donor plasma proteins
Development of a high-titer factor VIII autoantibody requiring management with a bypassing agent

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The combination of prolonged PT/PTT, hypofibrinogenemia, and thrombocytopenia reflects disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), defined in the sources as a consumption coagulopathy in which both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems become pathologically active simultaneously, consuming platelets and clotting factors faster than they can be replaced. The corresponding hemostatic support strategy is to monitor fibrinogen and platelets and transfuse platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate as indicated.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the source definition of DIC as a consumption coagulopathy with simultaneous pathologic procoagulant and fibrinolytic activation, explaining the combined factor and platelet depletion seen here.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: vitamin K deficiency impairs hepatic synthesis of functional factors II, VII, IX, and X (producing PIVKA proteins), which characteristically prolongs the PT without the concurrent thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenemia seen in this patient.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: a factor VIII autoantibody (inhibitor) causes isolated PTT prolongation from a single-factor deficiency and is managed with bypassing agents such as PCC or recombinant factor VIIa; it does not explain concurrent thrombocytopenia or hypofibrinogenemia.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: an anamnestic antibody response against a previously sensitized red cell antigen describes the mechanism of a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction, which produces hemolysis and anemia, not a global consumptive coagulopathy.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: antibody-mediated reactivity to donor plasma proteins describes the mechanism of an allergic/urticarial transfusion reaction, which produces hives rather than a coagulation factor and platelet consumption picture.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: During initial resuscitation of a major thermal injury, diffuse oozing accompanied by prolonged PT/PTT, hypofibrinogenemia, and thrombocytopenia points to DIC (simultaneous pathologic activation of procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems consuming platelets and clotting factors), a mechanism distinct from isolated factor-synthesis defects, factor inhibitors, or antibody-mediated transfusion reactions; hemostatic support is guided by serial fibrinogen and platelet monitoring with targeted component replacement.

source_references.supports: DIC is defined as a consumption coagulopathy with simultaneous pathologic activation of procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems, requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate; also source for vitamin K deficiency (PIVKA proteins) and factor VIII inhibitor mechanisms used in distractors B and C.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Nodes 31 (AHTR management), 32 (Non-Hemolytic Acute Reactions), 33 (Delayed Transfusion Reactions)
source_references.supports: Hemostatic support in suspected DIC involves monitoring fibrinogen and platelets and transfusing as indicated; also source for the anamnestic delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction mechanism (distractor D) and the antibody-mediated allergic reaction mechanism (distractor E).

review.notes: Confirmed via grep that no source text in any of the four approved sources ('burn'/'thermal' return zero hits across all four files) discusses burn/thermal-injury patients, burn resuscitation, or burn-associated coagulopathy/hemostatic agents. The blueprint node is specifically 'Patients with Thermal Injuries (Burns) > Initial Resuscitations > Hemostatic Agents,' but the question's clinical premise (a burn patient developing DIC during initial fluid resuscitation) is not stated or supported anywhere in the sources — it is an invented vignette bolted onto a generic, burn-unrelated DIC definitio

_audit.verdict_notes: Confirmed via grep that no source text in any of the four approved sources ('burn'/'thermal' return zero hits across all four files) discusses burn/thermal-injury patients, burn resuscitation, or burn-associated coagulopathy/hemostatic agents. The blueprint node is specifically 'Patients with Thermal Injuries (Burns) > Initial Resuscitations > Hemostatic Agents,' but the question's clinical premise (a burn patient developing DIC during initial fluid resuscitation) is not stated or supported anywhere in the sources — it is an invented vignette bolted onto a generic, burn-unrelated DIC definition. The underlying mechanism content itself is well grounded: SRC-002 Node 47 defines DIC almost verbatim as 'a consumption coagulopathy where both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically active; requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate,' and SRC-058 Node 31 supports 'if DIC is suspected, monitor fibrinogen and platelets, transfusing as indicated' (but that passage is in the context of AHTR workup, not burns). Distractor B (vitamin K deficiency/PIVKA) and C (factor VIII inhibitor/bypassing agents) are both accurately sourced to SRC-002 Node 47. Distractor D (anamnestic red cell antibody = DHTR mechanism) is accurately sourced to SRC-058 Node 33, and distractor E (antibody-mediated reaction to donor plasma proteins = allergic reaction) is accurately sourced to SRC-058 Node 32. Every individual mechanism claim is grounded, but the item does not actually test or source anything about burns or about 'hemostatic agents' as a treatment category (the node's actual subject) — it is a generic DIC-recognition question relabeled to fit the burn blueprint slot. Among the five given options, A is unambiguously the best answer since it is the only one consistent with the combined PT/PTT prolongation + hypofibrinogenemia + thrombocytopenia triad described; no other defensible choice exists given the answer set. The authors' own support_confidence of 'low' is consistent with this gap.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 38-year-old woman with blood group A receives a deceased-donor liver transplant from a blood group O donor because no ABO-identical organ is available in time. The perioperative course is uneventful with no signs of graft rejection. On postoperative day 9, her hemoglobin falls, indirect bilirubin rises, and the polyspecific direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive with reactivity confined to anti-IgG. An eluate prepared from her red cells reacts with A1 reagent red cells but is nonreactive with group O reagent red cells. Which of the following mechanisms best explains this patient's hemolysis?

options.text: Preformed recipient antibody directed against antigens on the donor graft, mediating antibody-mediated rejection of the transplanted liver
A cold-reactive IgM antibody with anti-I specificity fixing complement on the red cell membrane after cold exposure, producing a DAT reactive only with anti-C3
Nonspecific adsorption of a drug-antibody immune complex onto the red cell membrane, an eluate that is typically nonreactive unless the offending drug is added to the test system
Antibody-producing lymphocytes transferred within the ABO-nonidentical donor liver that continue to synthesize antibody directed against an antigen present on the recipient's own red cells
Autoantibody directed against an antigen shared by all reagent red cells, producing a panreactive eluate typical of warm-type immune hemolysis

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: This is minor ABO incompatibility (group O donor into a non-O recipient). Because the donor lacked the A antigen, per the reciprocal antibody relationship (Landsteiner's Rule) the donor possessed anti-A. Immunocompetent, antibody-producing lymphocytes carried within the transplanted liver persist after engraftment and continue to synthesize anti-A, which coats the recipient's own group A red cells. This produces an anti-IgG-positive DAT and an eluate reactive specifically with A1 cells (matching the recipient's own antigen type) rather than a panreactive, complement-only, or drug-dependent pattern, distinguishing it from warm autoimmune hemolysis, cold agglutinin-mediated hemolysis, and drug-induced immune hemolysis.

option_explanations.E: Incorrect: warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia classically produces a panreactive eluate against essentially all test red cells, not an eluate whose reactivity is confined to A1 cells and negative on group O cells.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: cold agglutinin syndrome produces a DAT that is positive for anti-C3 with a negative anti-IgG component and anti-I specificity; this patient's DAT reactivity is with anti-IgG, and the eluate shows anti-A rather than anti-I-type specificity.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia typically yields an eluate that is nonreactive unless the causative drug is added to the test system; here the eluate is clearly reactive with A1 cells without any drug addition, and no drug exposure is described.
option_explanations.D: Correct: donor-derived, antibody-producing lymphocytes transferred with the ABO-nonidentical (group O) graft continue producing anti-A, which binds the recipient's own group A red cells, consistent with the anti-IgG-positive DAT and the A1-specific eluate — the minor ABO incompatibility mechanism.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this describes major (not minor) ABO incompatibility, in which recipient antibody attacks donor graft antigens; it does not explain destruction of the recipient's own red cells, and in this case the donor is group O and expresses no A/B antigen for recipient antibody to target.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: In ABO-nonidentical solid organ transplantation, minor ABO incompatibility (group O donor into a non-O recipient) can lead to hemolysis of the recipient's own red cells because donor-derived, antibody-producing lymphocytes carried within the graft persist and synthesize isoagglutinin (e.g., anti-A) against the recipient's red cell antigens. This produces an antibody-specific, anti-IgG-positive DAT/eluate pattern that must be distinguished from warm autoimmune hemolysis (panreactive eluate), cold agglutinin-mediated hemolysis (anti-C3 only, anti-I specificity), drug-induced hemolysis (usually nonreactive eluate), and major ABO incompatibility (recipient antibody attacking the graft rather than causing recipient red cell destruction).

source_references.supports: Identifies 'serological management of Major vs. Minor ABO incompatibility' as a named high-yield transplantation immunohematology framework, used here as the mechanistic basis for the minor-incompatibility scenario (extrapolated from HSCT context to solid organ transplantation).

States the reciprocal antibody relationship (Landsteiner's Rule) — that individuals possess antibodies to the ABO antigens they lack — explaining why a group O donor (and cells transferred with the graft) would produce anti-A.

Describes DAT/eluate patterns distinguishing hemolysis mechanisms: WAIHA (anti-IgG positive DAT, panreactive eluate), cold agglutinin syndrome (anti-C3 positive/anti-IgG negative DAT, anti-I specificity), and drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia (typically nonreactive eluate unless drug is added) — used to construct the distractor rationales.

review.notes: Independently verified against all four sources. Two distinct sourcing problems: (1) Core mechanism is essentially unsupported: the ONLY source content touching this exact scenario is a single table-of-contents-style heading in SRC-041 line 17, 'Transplantation Immunohematology: Serological management of Major vs. Minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT' — a bare topic label with zero explanatory content, and it names HSCT (hematopoietic stem cell transplant), not solid organ transplantation (liver, as in the stem). SRC-041 has a full 'High-Yield Study Note' only for Subdomain Node 1 (ABO Discrepanc

_audit.verdict_notes: Independently verified against all four sources. Two distinct sourcing problems: (1) Core mechanism is essentially unsupported: the ONLY source content touching this exact scenario is a single table-of-contents-style heading in SRC-041 line 17, 'Transplantation Immunohematology: Serological management of Major vs. Minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT' — a bare topic label with zero explanatory content, and it names HSCT (hematopoietic stem cell transplant), not solid organ transplantation (liver, as in the stem). SRC-041 has a full 'High-Yield Study Note' only for Subdomain Node 1 (ABO Discrepancy); no study note exists for the transplantation node, so the passenger-lymphocyte mechanism, the donor-derived-lymphocyte-produces-antibody explanation, and its extension to solid-organ (vs. HSCT) transplantation appear nowhere in the four sources — they are extrapolated/invented relative to the provided material. The question's own source_references candidly label this 'extrapolated from HSCT context to solid organ transplantation' with only 'moderate' confidence, and the question sets support_confidence:'low' — consistent with thin/absent grounding, not full grounding. (2) Citation misattribution: the DAT/eluate comparison table (WAIHA panreactive eluate/anti-IgG+; CAS/PCH anti-C3+/anti-IgG-, anti-I or anti-P specificity; DIIHA nonreactive eluate unless drug added) is real and verbatim-matches lines 43-49, but those lines are in SRC-019 (clinical-foundations-of-immunohematology-and-transfusion-medicine.md), NOT SRC-004 (advanced-immunohematology-and-transfusion-medicine-review.md) as cited — SRC-004 contains no DAT/eluate/WAIHA/CAS/DIIHA content at all (its content covers molecular testing, PNH, HNA, cell kinetics, medicolegal topics). This is a clear source-ID error, not a minor locator slip. Landsteiner's Rule citation (SRC-041 lines 29-30) is accurate and correctly used. Despite the sourcing gaps, the clinical vignette itself is internally coherent and medically unambiguous: group O donor liver into group A recipient is textbook minor ABO incompatibility (passenger lymphocyte syndrome), and the DAT-anti-IgG-positive/eluate-reactive-with-A1-only pattern cleanly discriminates D from the other four options (A panreactive-warm, B anti-C3-only/cold, C drug-eluate-nonreactive, E wrong-direction major incompatibility descriptor). So independently I reach the same key (D) as the single best answer purely on domain reasoning, but that reasoning is not actually traceable to the four approved sources for the load-bearing facts.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 45-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease is scheduled to receive a living-donor kidney from an ABO- and HLA-incompatible donor. As part of the pretransplant desensitization protocol, she undergoes a series of therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) treatments to lower her circulating anti-A/anti-B isoagglutinin and donor-specific anti-HLA antibody levels before surgery. Which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism by which TPE reduces this patient's risk of antibody-mediated graft rejection?

options.text: TPE permanently depletes antibody-producing plasma cells, halting further antibody synthesis
TPE selectively binds and inactivates complement component C3, blocking the classical complement cascade

TPE physically removes preformed circulating antibodies from the plasma compartment via centrifugation or membrane filtration, lowering the antibody titer available to bind donor antigens on the graft
TPE gamma-irradiates donor T lymphocytes circulating in the recipient, preventing a graft-versus-host reaction
TPE eliminates donor CD34+ hematopoietic progenitor cells from the recipient's circulation to prevent chimerism

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Apheresis works on the principle of centrifugation or membrane filtration to separate and remove a targeted plasma component. The sources illustrate this same physical-removal mechanism in two other antibody/immune-complex-mediated conditions: TPE is a primary treatment for TTP because it removes the circulating ADAMTS13 inhibitor, and in rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis TPE is used to rapidly reduce the level of circulating immune complexes to prevent irreversible organ damage. Applying this same mechanistic principle to a desensitization protocol, TPE lowers preformed isoagglutinin and anti-HLA antibody titers by bulk removal from the plasma, reducing the antibody available to engage donor antigens on the graft and trigger antibody-mediated injury.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Direct complement inactivation is the mechanism of targeted pharmacologic agents such as eculizumab (used for acute, brisk hemolysis in cold agglutinin syndrome), not of TPE, which acts by bulk physical removal of plasma constituents rather than selective molecular inactivation of a complement protein.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Gamma irradiation of cellular blood components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes is the mechanism used to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD), an entirely different intervention from plasma exchange and not how TPE reduces antibody-mediated rejection risk.
option_explanations.C: Correct — see explanation_correct.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. CD34 is the antigen used to quantify hematopoietic progenitor cells in an HPC graft for hematopoietic (not solid organ) transplantation; TPE does not target or remove CD34+ cells, and this option confuses HPC transplant biology with the antibody-removal function of apheresis.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. This is a classic distractor: TPE removes antibody already present in the circulating plasma pool but does not deplete antibody-producing plasma cells or halt ongoing synthesis, which is why desensitization protocols typically pair TPE with agents such as rituximab that act on B cells to reduce antibody production going forward.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: TPE's core mechanism across all its indications (TTP, RPGN, and by extension antibody-mediated barriers to transplantation) is bulk physical removal of preformed circulating antibody or immune complexes via centrifugation/membrane filtration — it transiently lowers titer but does not stop ongoing antibody production, distinguishing it from complement-inhibitor therapy, B-cell/plasma-cell-targeted therapy, or cellular irradiation.

source_references.supports: Apheresis operates via centrifugation or membrane filtration, and TPE is a primary treatment for TTP specifically because it removes the circulating ADAMTS13 inhibitor — establishing the general mechanism of physical antibody/inhibitor removal from plasma.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 67 – Rheumatic & Renal Disorders in Apheresis
source_references.supports: In rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, TPE is used to rapidly reduce circulating immune complex levels to prevent irreversible organ damage, illustrating the same physical-removal rationale applied to prevent antibody/immune-complex-mediated tissue injury.
TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, a distinct mechanism used to ground the incorrect irradiation distractor.

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain I – Clinical Practice, Management section
source_references.supports: Eculizumab (complement inhibition) and rituximab (B-cell targeting to reduce antibody production) are described as management options distinct from TPE, supporting the distractors about complement inactivation and permanent plasma-cell depletion.

review.notes: Confirmed by independent read of all four sources: none of SRC-010, SRC-011, SRC-012, or SRC-019 contain ANY statement about TPE/plasma exchange used for ABO- or HLA-incompatible solid organ transplant desensitization, isoagglutinin/anti-HLA antibody reduction pre-transplant, or antibody-mediated graft rejection prevention (searched 'transplant', 'isoagglutinin', 'donor-specific', 'graft rejection', 'desensitiz', 'ABO-incompatible', 'HLA-incompatible' across all four files — the only 'transplant' hits are an unrelated HPC-transplantation domain heading and a chimerism blurb). This is precisely

_audit.verdict_notes: Confirmed by independent read of all four sources: none of SRC-010, SRC-011, SRC-012, or SRC-019 contain ANY statement about TPE/plasma exchange used for ABO- or HLA-incompatible solid organ transplant desensitization, isoagglutinin/anti-HLA antibody reduction pre-transplant, or antibody-mediated graft rejection prevention (searched 'transplant', 'isoagglutinin', 'donor-specific', 'graft rejection', 'desensitiz', 'ABO-incompatible', 'HLA-incompatible' across all four files — the only 'transplant' hits are an unrelated HPC-transplantation domain heading and a chimerism blurb). This is precisely the blueprint node being tested (S10.d.ii.c/F: Plasma Exchange for ABO/HLA immunologic barriers in solid organ transplant), yet the sources are silent on it. The question instead stitches together the mechanism fact (SRC-010: apheresis works via centrifugation/membrane filtration; TPE removes ADAMTS13 inhibitor in TTP) with an analogous but different indication (SRC-011 Subdomain Node 67: TPE reduces circulating immune complexes in RPGN) and then extrapolates by general medical knowledge, not source text, to the transplant-desensitization scenario. The explanation itself concedes this ('Applying this same mechanistic principle to a desensitization protocol...') and the question's own support_confidence is self-rated 'low', which matches my finding. The distractor-grounding facts (TA-GVHD prevented by irradiation of donor T cells — SRC-010 Domain V; CD34 quantifies HPC grafts — SRC-010 Domain VIII; eculizumab used for acute brisk hemolysis in CAS, rituximab as CAS/WAIHA therapy — SRC-019) are each individually well supported, but none of them establish TPE's actual mechanistic role in transplant desensitization — they are used only to eliminate distractors by contrast, not to positively source the correct-answer premise. Despite this grounding gap, the mechanism described in option C (bulk physical removal of antibody via centrifugation/membrane filtration) is medically unambiguous and is the only physically/mechanistically coherent option among A/B/D/E, so the keyed answer is not in genuine clinical dispute — I independently select C as well, and no other option is defensible. The core issue is source support for the vignette's premise, not the correctness of the answer choice.

questions_needing_review.stem: A hematopathology laboratory performs flow cytometric immunophenotyping on a peripheral blood specimen submitted to evaluate for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH). The assay is designed to enumerate the percentage of granulocytes that fail to express two glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI)-anchored surface proteins, CD55 and CD59, so the laboratory can quantify the abnormal clone as a percentage of the total granulocyte population. Which of the following underlying cellular mechanisms best explains why flow cytometry is able to identify and enumerate this double-negative population?

options.text: An acquired somatic mutation in the PIGA gene impairs synthesis of the GPI anchor, preventing CD55 and CD59 from being attached to the cell membrane
Complement-mediated lysis selectively destroys granulocytes that still express CD55 and CD59, leaving only antigen-negative cells to be counted
A deletion of the RHD gene eliminates a glycoprotein scaffold shared with CD55 and CD59, preventing their membrane expression
Prestorage leukoreduction filtration removes CD55/CD59-positive granulocytes before the sample reaches the flow cytometer
Circulating alloantibodies bind and strip CD55 and CD59 from the granulocyte surface before the sample is analyzed

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: PNH results from an acquired clonal mutation in the PIGA gene in a hematopoietic stem cell, which impairs synthesis of the GPI anchor. Because CD55 and CD59 (along with Yta/Ytb, Hy/Joa/Gya, Cromer antigens, and Emm) are GPI-linked, the mutant clone's progeny cannot display these proteins on the membrane. Flow cytometry is the modern gold-standard method that directly identifies and quantifies this GPI-anchor-deficient (CD55/CD59 double-negative) population, which is the mechanistic basis for using flow cytometry as an enumeration tool in PNH.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the source explicitly identifies an acquired PIGA gene mutation causing GPI anchor deficiency as the defect underlying loss of GPI-linked antigens including CD55 and CD59, which flow cytometry is used to quantify.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect distractor: PNH is described as an intrinsic membrane-synthesis defect, not an antibody-mediated process; no alloantibody-mediated stripping of CD55/CD59 is described for PNH in the source material.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect distractor mixing systems: RHD gene deletion is the mechanism producing the D-negative Rh phenotype, an entirely separate gene and antigen system with no described relationship to GPI-anchor biosynthesis or CD55/CD59 expression.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: complement-mediated lysis (via loss of the complement regulators CD55/CD59) is described as the clinical consequence of GPI-anchor deficiency causing chronic intravascular hemolysis, not the reason the antigen-negative population is detectable by flow cytometry; flow cytometry directly measures antigen absence, not selective lysis of antigen-positive cells.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: leukoreduction filtration is described as reducing WBC content of blood components to lower the risk of primary HLA alloimmunization and is unrelated to PNH clone detection or GPI-anchor biology.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Flow cytometric enumeration of a defined cell population relies on antibody-based detection of surface antigen presence or absence; in PNH this reflects an intrinsic GPI-anchor synthesis defect (PIGA mutation) rather than an immune, filtration, or unrelated-gene mechanism. Note: the approved source set does not contain direct content on CD34+ HPC enumeration by flow cytometry (e.g., ISHAGE gating, viability assessment); this question uses the closest source-grounded flow-cytometric cell-enumeration mechanism (PNH clone quantification) as an analog, hence the low support-confidence rating.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 42 – PNH: Core Concepts / Antigen Deficiencies in PNH / Diagnostic Logic
source_references.supports: PIGA gene mutation causes GPI anchor deficiency; CD55/CD59 are GPI-linked and absent in PNH; flow cytometry is the modern gold-standard method used to quantify the population of cells lacking CD55 and CD59.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 2 – Rh Variants & RhIg: Core Concepts / Genetic Basis
source_references.supports: RHD gene deletion is the mechanism of the D-negative Rh phenotype, used here as an unrelated distractor gene/antigen system.
Leukoreduction reduces WBC content to lower HLA alloimmunization risk, used as an unrelated distractor mechanism.

review.notes: Core mechanistic facts are grounded in SRC-004 Node 42 (PIGA mutation -> GPI anchor deficiency -> loss of CD55/CD59 -> flow cytometry as gold-standard quantification method), and the RHD-deletion and leukoreduction/HLA-alloimmunization distractor facts are correctly sourced from SRC-020 and SRC-026 respectively. Option A is unambiguously the only defensible answer; B, C, D, E are each directly contradicted or unrelated per source content (D in particular inverts the source's actual mechanism, since complement lyses the CD55/CD59-NEGATIVE cells, not the positive ones). However, this item has a

_audit.verdict_notes: Core mechanistic facts are grounded in SRC-004 Node 42 (PIGA mutation -> GPI anchor deficiency -> loss of CD55/CD59 -> flow cytometry as gold-standard quantification method), and the RHD-deletion and leukoreduction/HLA-alloimmunization distractor facts are correctly sourced from SRC-020 and SRC-026 respectively. Option A is unambiguously the only defensible answer; B, C, D, E are each directly contradicted or unrelated per source content (D in particular inverts the source's actual mechanism, since complement lyses the CD55/CD59-NEGATIVE cells, not the positive ones). However, this item has a critical alignment problem: the blueprint node it is filed under (16. HPC Transplantation > HPC Assessment > HPC Measurement and Enumeration > Flow Cytometry) concerns CD34+ hematopoietic progenitor cell enumeration for stem-cell products (e.g., ISHAGE gating, viability/7-AAD assessment, dual-platform counting) -- a completely different clinical/technical domain from PNH clone detection. The question's own teaching_point explicitly admits the source set contains no content on CD34+ HPC enumeration and that PNH clone quantification is being used merely as an 'analog' flow-cytometry mechanism. This means the item does not actually test the assigned blueprint topic at all; it tests an unrelated diagnostic application of flow cytometry from a different subdomain (PNH/immunohematology, SRC-004 Node 42), which was itself explicitly flagged in the source document as one of several 'remaining' topics outside the main subdomain structure. Additionally, the stem's specific framing of 'granulocytes' as the analyzed cell population is not stated in the source, which frames PNH antigen loss in terms of RBC-membrane GPI-anchored antigens (Yta/Ytb, Hy/Joa/Gya, Cromer, CD59, Emm) -- granulocyte-based clinical flow testing is real-world accurate but goes beyond what SRC-004 states, so this is an added, source-unsupported specific detail layered onto an already off-topic item. Recommend rejecting or reassigning this item; it should not be used to certify coverage of the HPC Measurement/Enumeration/Flow Cytometry blueprint node.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 54-year-old woman with AML in remission undergoes myeloablative allogeneic HSCT from her HLA-identical sibling. The donor is blood group O; the recipient is blood group A (major ABO incompatibility), and the marrow graft underwent red cell reduction prior to infusion per protocol. On post-transplant day +18, her absolute neutrophil count has recovered to 800/µL and the platelet count is trending upward, but she remains transfusion-dependent for red cells. Reticulocyte count is <1%, and a bone marrow biopsy shows normal myeloid maturation with absent erythroid precursors. Which of the following best explains this patient's isolated failure of red cell engraftment?

options.text: DMSO-mediated cytotoxicity to the infused cryopreserved stem cells
An inadequate CD34+ cell dose was infused, resulting in insufficient stem cell engraftment
Graft-versus-host disease with donor T-cell-mediated destruction of the marrow stroma
Persistent recipient isoagglutinins (anti-A) are lysing developing donor-derived erythroid precursors in the marrow
Host-versus-graft rejection driven by HLA disparity between donor and recipient

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: This is pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) following major ABO-mismatched HSCT, which occurs in 3%-29% of such transplants. The recipient's preexisting isoagglutinins (here, anti-A, since the group-A recipient received a group-O graft) persist after transplant and lyse the developing donor-derived (group A) erythroid precursors in the marrow. Because these antibodies target red cell antigens specifically, myeloid and megakaryocytic lineages engraft normally (as seen here with ANC recovery and rising platelets) while erythropoiesis fails, producing reticulocytopenia (<1%) and absent erythroid precursors on marrow biopsy despite otherwise successful engraftment.

option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the described mechanism of PRCA in major ABO-mismatched HSCT-recipient isoagglutinins lyse donor RBC precursors, sparing myeloid and platelet engraftment since those lineages lack the targeted ABO antigens.
option_explanations.C: GVHD requires immunosuppression to prevent donor T-cells from attacking host tissue, but it classically involves skin, gut, and liver and would not selectively destroy only erythroid precursors while sparing myeloid recovery; it does not explain this lineage-restricted pattern.
option_explanations.E: HLA disparity is the primary barrier to engraftment and drives graft rejection, but this patient received an HLA-identical sibling graft, and true rejection would impair all hematopoietic lineages rather than causing an isolated erythroid defect.
option_explanations.B: The recognized threshold for successful engraftment is a CD34+ dose of roughly 2-6 x 10^6 cells/kg; an inadequate dose would cause global (all-lineage) delayed or failed engraftment, not a selective absence of erythroid precursors with normal myeloid recovery.
option_explanations.A: DMSO toxicity is an infusion-related reaction (coughing, flushing, rash, cardiovascular instability) occurring at the time cryopreserved product is administered; it is not a cause of delayed marrow engraftment failure occurring weeks later.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Isolated failure of red cell engraftment with normal myeloid/platelet recovery after major ABO-mismatched HSCT points to PRCA caused by persistent recipient isoagglutinins lysing donor erythroid precursors, distinct from global graft failure mechanisms like HLA mismatch rejection, GVHD, or inadequate CD34+ dose.

source_references.supports: States PRCA occurs in 3%-29% of major ABO-mismatched transplants, characterized by reticulocytes <1% and absence of erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment; also defines the HLA barrier and the ~2x10^6 CD34+/kg engraftment dosing goal used in distractors.

States PRCA in HSCT occurs in 3%-29% of major ABO-mismatched transplants and that recipient antibodies (isoagglutinins) lyse developing donor RBC precursors in the marrow, directly grounding the mechanism in the correct answer; also describes DMSO toxicity symptoms used in a distractor.
Confirms major/minor ABO incompatibility management and HLA matching are core HPC transplantation concepts, providing general topic context.

review.notes: General PRCA mechanism (3-29% of major ABO-mismatched HSCT, retic <1%, absent erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment, recipient isoagglutinins lysing donor-derived RBC precursors) is verbatim supported by SRC-038 and SRC-040. However, the specific ABO setup in the stem is internally wrong: donor group O / recipient group A is, by standard definition and by the sources' own worked examples, a MINOR ABO mismatch (SRC-038 defines Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome using exactly this donor-O/recipient-A pattern, and states minor mismatch is managed by plasma reduction, not the 'red cell reduc

_audit.verdict_notes: General PRCA mechanism (3-29% of major ABO-mismatched HSCT, retic <1%, absent erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment, recipient isoagglutinins lysing donor-derived RBC precursors) is verbatim supported by SRC-038 and SRC-040. However, the specific ABO setup in the stem is internally wrong: donor group O / recipient group A is, by standard definition and by the sources' own worked examples, a MINOR ABO mismatch (SRC-038 defines Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome using exactly this donor-O/recipient-A pattern, and states minor mismatch is managed by plasma reduction, not the 'red cell reduction' the stem describes, which the source ties specifically to major mismatch). A group-A recipient has anti-B, not anti-A, antibodies (basic ABO self-tolerance), so the explanation's claim of persistent recipient 'anti-A' isoagglutinins is factually impossible for this patient. The explanation also self-contradicts the stem by calling the target cells 'donor-derived (group A) erythroid precursors' when the donor is stated to be group O. The item needs the ABO group assignment (or the major-mismatch framing/antibody specificity) corrected. Despite this flaw, among the five given options A remains the only one that fits the isolated-erythroid-failure marrow picture; B, C, D, and E are each well-contradicted by the sources (HLA-identical sibling rules out C; GVHD/DMSO/CD34-dose mechanisms don't produce a lineage-restricted erythroid defect per SRC-038/SRC-040), so I independently also select A, but flag the underlying rationale as internally inconsistent and factually flawed as written.

questions_needing_review.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions_needing_review.stem: A 52-year-old man with multiple myeloma is scheduled for autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. His hematopoietic progenitor cells could be collected from any of three possible sources: bone marrow aspirate, mobilized peripheral blood via apheresis, or (in an allogeneic setting) banked umbilical cord blood. Before the product is cryopreserved, the processing laboratory must quantify the hematopoietic stem/progenitor cell content of the graft to confirm it is adequate for engraftment, using a single cell-surface marker that is expressed on immature progenitor cells common to all three of these sources. Which antigen is used for this quantification?

options.text: CD3
HLA-DR
CD45
CD34
CD133

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Regardless of whether the graft is collected from bone marrow, mobilized peripheral blood, or umbilical cord blood, the laboratory identifies and enumerates the hematopoietic progenitor cell population using CD34, which the source curriculum designates as the primary antigen used to quantify HPCs in a graft prior to processing and cryopreservation.

option_explanations.A: CD3 marks T lymphocytes and is relevant to assessing graft T-cell content (a factor in graft-versus-host disease risk), not to quantifying the hematopoietic progenitor cell dose itself.
option_explanations.D: Correct. CD34 is the primary antigen used to quantify hematopoietic progenitor cells in a graft, serving as the common source-independent marker across marrow, peripheral blood, and cord blood products.
option_explanations.C: CD45 is a pan-leukocyte marker expressed broadly on white blood cells and is not specific for the immature progenitor population that defines graft adequacy.
option_explanations.E: CD133 is a distractor representing another stem cell-associated marker, but the source material specifies CD34, not CD133, as the primary antigen used for HPC graft quantification.
option_explanations.B: HLA-DR is a class II MHC molecule used in donor-recipient matching and antigen presentation, not in enumerating the hematopoietic progenitor cell dose of a graft.

source_references.supports: States CD34 is the primary antigen used to quantify HPCs in a graft, within the Domain VIII subdomain covering HPC Sources (Marrow, Peripheral Blood, Cord).

review.notes: Only one atomic fact is actually grounded: SRC-010 line 113 states verbatim 'CD34 is the primary antigen used to quantify HPCs in a graft' under Domain VIII (HPC Transplantation), subdomain 'Sources (Marrow, Peripheral Blood, Cord)'. That single line supports the keyed answer B (CD34) and the core explanation_correct claim. However: (1) none of the four distractor rationales are sourced anywhere in the four approved documents — CD3/T-cell content/GVHD-risk, CD45 pan-leukocyte marker, CD133 as 'another stem cell marker', and HLA-DR's role in donor-recipient matching are never mentioned in SRC-0

_audit.verdict_notes: Only one atomic fact is actually grounded: SRC-010 line 113 states verbatim 'CD34 is the primary antigen used to quantify HPCs in a graft' under Domain VIII (HPC Transplantation), subdomain 'Sources (Marrow, Peripheral Blood, Cord)'. That single line supports the keyed answer B (CD34) and the core explanation_correct claim. However: (1) none of the four distractor rationales are sourced anywhere in the four approved documents — CD3/T-cell content/GVHD-risk, CD45 pan-leukocyte marker, CD133 as 'another stem cell marker', and HLA-DR's role in donor-recipient matching are never mentioned in SRC-010, SRC-002, SRC-006, or SRC-001 (verified via targeted grep across all four files: zero hits for CD133, CD45, CD3, or HLA-DR-matching in any HPC/stem-cell context). These rationales are true from general hematology knowledge but are invented relative to the cited sources. (2) The stem's specific claim that CD34 marks progenitor cells 'common to all three of these sources' is an inference beyond the literal source text, which only lists Marrow/Peripheral Blood/Cord as subdomain headings alongside the CD34 fact without explicitly stating cross-source marker commonality. (3) The elaborate clinical vignette (52-year-old man, multiple myeloma, autologous HSCT candidate) is invented scenario-dressing with no source basis. (4) Possible blueprint mismatch: the blueprint node targets Domain 17 'Transplant Organizations and Networks in the Regulation of Cellular and Tissue Therapy Programs' (an administrative/regulatory topic — e.g., FACT/AABB/NMDP accreditation of HPC source facilities), but the actual grounding fact comes from Domain VIII 'HPC Transplantation' (a biology/lab-processing domain covering Biology, Sources, Processing, Storage, Engraftment) — a different domain than the administration/regulatory one named in the blueprint topic string. The question tests a lab marker fact, not organizational/regulatory content about HPC sources. Despite these grounding gaps, CD34 is unambiguously the correct real-world answer for HPC graft quantification, so the keyed answer itself is not in dispute — this is a sourcing-rigor problem, not a correctness problem. The candidate's own metadata (support_confidence: low) already signals this weakness.

cases_needing_review.title: Suspected Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction: Investigation Pathway
domains: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
cases_needing_review.clinical_setup: A 54-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia is admitted to the oncology ward for symptomatic anemia and receives a single unit of crossmatched RBCs. Shortly after the transfusion is completed, the bedside nurse reports concern to the transfusion service; the infusion is stopped, and a post-transfusion specimen along with the implicated unit and attached tubing are sent to the blood bank for work-up under the facility's nonconforming-event (NCE) process.

findings.label: Reason for transfusion
findings.value: Hgb 7.0 g/dL, symptomatic anemia in a patient with AML (illustrative)

findings.label: Repeat Hgb after 1 unit RBC
findings.value: 7.1 g/dL — essentially no rise (expected roughly 8.0 g/dL, i.e., ~1 g/dL increment per unit transfused)

findings.label: Clerical/identification record review
findings.value: Discrepancy found — specimen/patient identification mismatch at collection

findings.label: Implicated unit and attached tubing
findings.value: Quarantined in-house pending investigation (not discarded, not reissued)

findings.label: Biological Product Deviation (BPD) criteria assessment
findings.value: All 5 criteria met: manufacturing-associated, deviation from specification, potential safety/purity/potency impact, occurred under facility control, product distributed/administered

decision_points.prompt: The bedside nurse stops the infusion and notifies the transfusion service of a possible reaction, sending the patient's post-transfusion specimen and the implicated unit/tubing to the lab. Before any test result is available, what must the transfusion service do first under the nonconforming-event (NCE) framework?
options: Discard the unit immediately so it cannot be reissued
Document the event, determine its potential impact on product safety, and quarantine the remaining unit/tubing in-house
Wait until the DAT result returns before taking any action
Immediately file a Biological Product Deviation report with the FDA
decision_points.answer: Document the event, determine its potential impact on product safety, and quarantine the remaining unit/tubing in-house.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-027's NCE management sequence begins with Documentation (capture the facts, classify by risk severity) and Impact Determination (evaluate effect on the product's safety, purity, or potency), followed immediately by Quarantine/Recall — securing affected product in-house. This mirrors the 'quarantine of products' and 'investigation of manufacturing deficiencies' safety layers in SRC-013. Discarding the unit or waiting for lab results would destroy evidence needed for investigation; BPD reporting only comes later, after root cause analysis confirms the criteria are met.

decision_points.prompt: With the unit quarantined and the event documented, which single laboratory test on the post-transfusion specimen is emphasized as the key tool to determine whether the reaction has an immune-mediated hemolytic basis?
options: Peripheral blood smear for schistocytes
Direct antiglobulin test (DAT)
HLA class I antibody screen
One-time T. cruzi antibody screen
decision_points.rationale: SRC-027 notes that current transfusion-medicine practice (Ch. 14 update) 'focuses on the positive DAT' as the hallmark of immune-mediated hemolysis. A positive DAT on the post-transfusion specimen indicates antibody and/or complement coating red cells, supporting an immune-mediated hemolytic process.

decision_points.prompt: DAT returns positive, and the repeat Hgb shows essentially no rise from baseline despite transfusion of a full unit (well short of the expected ~1 g/dL increment). What is the next required step in the investigation?
options: Close the event since the DAT already explains the reaction
Interview staff and review records (collection, labeling, compatibility testing, issue) to reconstruct the event, then perform root cause analysis
Proceed directly to CAPA without reviewing records
Refer the case only to the ordering physician with no quality-system follow-up
decision_points.answer: Interview staff and review records to reconstruct the event, then perform root cause analysis to identify the underlying, correctable cause.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-027's NCE steps place Investigation (interview staff, review records) and Root Cause Analysis (identify the underlying, controllable cause) after impact determination/quarantine and before CAPA. The inadequate Hgb increment, measured against the ~1 g/dL-per-unit benchmark from SRC-006, is objective evidence that transfused red cells are not surviving in circulation, reinforcing the need for full investigation rather than closing the event on the DAT alone.

decision_points.prompt: Record review uncovers a patient/specimen identification discrepancy at collection, and the implicated unit had already been fully administered before the error was recognized. Does this event meet the criteria for mandatory Biological Product Deviation (BPD) reporting to the FDA?
options: No — clerical errors are never reportable
No — reporting is only required if the patient dies
Yes — all five BPD criteria are satisfied
Reporting is discretionary and left to the medical director
decision_points.answer: Yes — all five BPD criteria are satisfied.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-027 lists five criteria that must all be met for mandatory BPD reporting: associated with manufacturing (including collection/labeling), a deviation from established specifications, may affect safety/purity/potency, occurred while the facility had control of the product, and involved a product that was distributed (here, administered to the patient). Because the identification error meets all five, the transfusion service must report the BPD, complete root cause analysis and CAPA, and conduct an effectiveness review.

cases_needing_review.answer_key: Suspected reaction → stop administration; send post-transfusion specimen + implicated unit/tubing to the transfusion service → Document the event, determine impact on product safety, and quarantine the remaining unit/tubing in-house (NCE Documentation + Quarantine/Recall step, mirroring the 'quarantine of products' safety layer) → Perform DAT on the post-transfusion specimen to test for immune-mediated hemolysis, and check the Hgb increment against the ~1 g/dL-per-unit benchmark → Investigate: interview staff and review records (collection, labeling, compatibility testing, issue) → Root Cause Analysis identifies a patient/specimen identification error → Because all 5 BPD criteria are met (manufacturing-associated, deviation from specification, potential safety/purity/potency impact, occurred under facility control, product distributed/administered), file a Biological Product Deviation report to the FDA, complete CAPA, and conduct an effectiveness review.
cases_needing_review.teaching_explanation: This case links two threads the exam blueprint treats separately but that converge in real practice: the serologic/hematologic evidence of hemolysis and the quality-system response that 'laboratory investigation' of a hemolytic transfusion reaction actually requires. SRC-010 frames hemolytic reactions (acute and delayed) as a distinct Hazards-of-Transfusion subdomain whose workup and prevention differ from TRALI, TACO, and TA-GVHD. Once a reaction is suspected, SRC-027's nonconforming-event framework governs the response: document and classify the event, determine impact on product safety, and quarantine the implicated unit and tubing in-house — echoing the 'quarantine of products' and 'investigation of manufacturing deficiencies' layers of blood safety in SRC-013. The DAT (SRC-027, Ch.14 immune-mediated hemolysis update) is the pivotal serologic test for immune-mediated destruction, while the expected ~1 g/dL-per-unit hemoglobin increment (SRC-006) gives an objective, quantitative signal that transfused red cells are not surviving in circulation. When investigation and root cause analysis (SRC-027) trace the event to a patient/specimen misidentification, all five Biological Product Deviation criteria are satisfied, mandating FDA reporting alongside CAPA and an effectiveness review.
pitfalls: Quarantine (securing an implicated product still in-house) and recall (retrieving product already distributed elsewhere) are distinct NCE actions in SRC-027 — do not use the terms interchangeably on the exam.

A positive DAT documents immune-mediated red cell coating/destruction but is not itself the root cause; SRC-027's investigation step (staff interview plus record review) is what identifies the underlying, correctable cause such as a specimen/patient identification error.

Biological Product Deviation reporting is not automatic just because an error occurred — SRC-027 requires all five criteria to be met simultaneously, including that the product was already distributed; an error caught before the unit left the transfusion service would not meet that criterion.

SRC-010 separates acute and delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions as distinct entities with their own incidence, pathophysiology, and prevention considerations — do not assume the same workup and reporting timeline applies uniformly to both.

source_references.supports: Hemolytic transfusion reactions (acute and delayed) form a distinct subdomain of the Hazards-of-Transfusion domain, separate from TRALI/TACO/TA-GVHD, with incidence, pathophysiology, and prevention differing between acute and delayed forms.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts – Five Layers of Safety
source_references.supports: Quarantine of implicated products and investigation of manufacturing/process deficiencies are core layers of the blood-safety response framework invoked once a suspected reaction or error is identified.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 65 – Intravascular Cell Kinetics & Expected Increments
source_references.supports: Expected RBC increment (~1 g/dL Hgb, or ~3% Hct, per unit transfused) is the benchmark used to recognize an inadequate post-transfusion response consistent with ongoing red cell destruction.
A positive DAT is the key serologic marker emphasized for identifying immune-mediated red cell hemolysis (Ch.14 Immune-Mediated Hemolysis/AIHA update).

Nonconforming-event management proceeds through documentation, impact determination, quarantine/recall, investigation (staff interview plus record review), root cause analysis, CAPA, reporting, and effectiveness review.

Biological Product Deviation reporting to the FDA is mandatory only when all five criteria are met: manufacturing-associated, deviation from cGMP/specification, potential safety/purity/potency impact, occurred under facility control, and involves a distributed product.

review.notes:
Verified against all four sources directly.
